MP Materials said on Wednesday it has published production guidance for the first quarter, outlining expectations for output at its rare earths processing operations in the United States.

The company, which produces and processes neodymium and praseodymium used in permanent magnets, forecast Q1 production based on expected performance across its mining, separation and downstream processing activities. The guidance is intended to help investors gauge expected volumes as MP Materials works to supply magnet makers and other customers seeking rare earth inputs.

MP Materials’ update follows the release of its prior-period results and comes as the market continues to track supply from both new and established producers amid ongoing efforts to secure rare earth supply chains. The company’s quarterly outlook reflects operating factors including feed quality, plant throughput, maintenance and inventory flows, according to the guidance details it provided.

In its quarterly reporting, MP Materials has previously emphasized that production levels can vary with ore characteristics and the timing of processing campaigns at its Mountain Pass site in California. The rare earth concentrate mined at the site is processed into intermediate products and then further refined, with production influenced by how much material is available for each step and the operational run rates at the separation facilities.

A company spokesperson said in connection with the guidance that MP Materials “expects Q1 production to reflect current operating conditions and planned throughput at our facilities,” adding that it would continue to update markets as it refines its expectations during the quarter.

MP Materials has been expanding its processing capacity and has also been working to support the production of high-grade rare earth compounds used in magnet manufacturing. The company’s guidance provides a baseline for anticipated output of separated products and related materials, which are sold to customers under supply agreements and spot transactions.

Investors have closely monitored MP Materials’ ability to sustain production volumes and manage costs, particularly as demand for rare earth magnet materials is tied to industries such as electric vehicles, wind turbines and defense systems. The guidance update is likely to be followed by further disclosures as the quarter progresses and the company reports actual results.
bodies_by_headline_hash.742d98b3c41f2b13bf2f:
Long-term demand for rare-earth elements is likely to depend heavily on the pace of electric-vehicle adoption, with analysts pointing to EV-related magnets as the key driver for several of the materials used in motors and generators.

Rare earths such as neodymium and praseodymium are widely used in permanent magnet motors, which are becoming increasingly important as automakers shift toward battery-electric vehicles. Demand for the magnets tends to rise with EV penetration because a typical EV drivetrain uses significantly more rare-earth magnet material than many conventional powertrains. Analysts said that, beyond near-term government incentives, the trajectory of EV sales will determine whether growth in rare-earth requirements accelerates or plateaus.

A separate set of rare-earth applications includes wind turbines and industrial equipment, though EVs are seen as the largest incremental source of consumption over the coming decade. Magnet demand also intersects with policy on vehicle emissions, battery supply chains and grid expansion, but EV volumes remain the most direct link between consumption and industrial adoption. Market forecasters have increasingly modelled rare-earth demand under different EV penetration scenarios, ranging from slower transitions to faster scaling supported by expanding charging infrastructure and cost reductions.

In addition, supply constraints for mining and separation capacity can affect whether demand can be met even if EV growth is strong. Rare-earth processing is concentrated in a limited number of countries, leaving downstream manufacturers sensitive to disruptions and trade policies. Analysts said that tighter margins can emerge when magnet producers need to secure feedstock or qualified processing capacity months or years ahead of production schedules.

“EV penetration is the swing factor because it directly determines the number of high-performance traction motors being built,” one industry analyst said, adding that wind and other sectors provide additional support but do not offset EV changes in the medium term.

The outlook is further complicated by material intensity trends. Motor designs can vary in the amount of rare-earth content per unit of output, and companies continue to work on magnet efficiency, recycling and substitution. However, analysts said that scaling EV manufacturing at current technology levels would still likely require substantial magnet output, even with incremental improvements in material use.

Producers and magnet makers have also started building more robust procurement strategies, including longer-term offtake agreements and investment in processing assets where possible. Still, the central demand question for the next phase of the rare-earth market remains how quickly EV sales expand and whether policy-led growth translates into sustained, large-scale manufacturing of electrified vehicles.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ef69167530b48af820fa:
Sandvik on Sunday raised its guidance for the full fiscal year, citing improving demand in parts of the mining sector and a stronger order book, the Swedish industrial group said.

The company increased its outlook for adjusted operating profit (EBIT) and revenue for the year, as it reported that performance had strengthened since its previous forecast. Sandvik said the changes reflect better-than-expected trading in its Mining and Rock Technology segment as customers continue equipment spending after a period of cautious purchasing.

In its update, Sandvik pointed to a mix of stabilizing volumes and ongoing project activity among miners, particularly where customers have focused on productivity and energy efficiency. The company also cited improved delivery schedules and the benefits of actions taken to manage costs and supply-chain constraints.

Sandvik, which makes drilling equipment, tools and automation systems for mining and construction customers, said it continued to expect that demand will remain uneven by region and customer. The company said it remains watchful of macroeconomic conditions and the timing of large orders, which can affect quarterly results in the equipment cycle.

“Based on our improved order intake and execution, we are raising our full-year guidance,” Sandvik Chief Executive Officer Stefan Widing said in a statement. He added that the company expects continued progress across its key businesses, while acknowledging that market conditions remain variable.

The guidance raise comes as mining companies navigate commodity price fluctuations and continue to balance investment plans with cash flow. Sandvik has also been benefiting from broader adoption of digital solutions and services that help customers optimize equipment uptime and operating costs.

Sandvik’s stock has fluctuated in recent months as investors weighed expectations for demand against uncertainty in industrial spending. The company did not provide additional details on the magnitude of the guidance increase beyond its fiscal targets, and it said it will give a fuller update in its next scheduled reporting period.
bodies_by_headline_hash.3bb477298a3371845f48:
Cobalt prices stabilized after a recent drawdown, with trading activity steady as buyers recalibrated orders following the latest pullback, market sources said on Tuesday.

Fastmarkets assessed that spot cobalt prices moved within a narrower range than in the previous sessions, reflecting a pause in selling pressure after declines earlier in the week. Several traders said near-term availability remained sufficient, but that expectations for demand from battery supply chains improved on the margin as importers continued to secure material for scheduled production runs.

Dealers attributed the stabilization to a combination of reduced spot replenishment and more balanced interest between sellers and buyers. While some producers offered material at lower levels to clear inventory, others held offers, citing uncertainty around end-market demand and the timing of replenishment cycles for both chemical and battery-grade products.

“The market is finding a level where physical sellers are not forced to cut further, and buyers are willing to top up,” one cobalt trader said, adding that volumes were “not heavy, but consistent.” The trader said that participants were watching for signals from upstream nickel and lithium markets, which have tended to influence pricing sentiment for battery metals.

In recent weeks, cobalt has been affected by fluctuating expectations for electric vehicle and battery production growth, along with movements in broader base-metal pricing. Market participants said the latest drawdown had already been partially digested, reducing the urgency for additional hedging among some spot buyers. At the same time, several supply contracts are expected to be repriced or renegotiated later in the quarter, keeping parts of the market cautious.

Fastmarkets sources also noted that market liquidity has been uneven across delivery periods, with some auctions and bilateral deals showing tighter spreads for immediate shipments compared with later tenors. Trading desks said they expect continued range-bound conditions unless there is a sharper shift in buying schedules or additional changes to producer supply.

Further price guidance will depend on incoming purchase orders, freight and warehousing costs, and any updates from major refining hubs. With spot pricing currently stabilizing, participants said the market is likely to remain focused on short-term physical availability as buyers plan for the next phase of replenishment.
bodies_by_headline_hash.79bc89c808705946e483:
China has released its 2025 rare-earth production quota unchanged from the previous year, maintaining limits on output of the minerals widely used in magnets, wind turbines and electric vehicle motors, according to an industry notice published on Wednesday.

The quota covers rare earth production in 2025 for both regulated output categories and other planned volumes, and it keeps the overall cap at the same level as in 2024, according to the document shared with companies and posted by domestic industry sources. The release comes ahead of the government’s annual work on resource planning and is intended to guide output across mines and downstream processing.

Rare earths are subject to state management in China through production indicators and allocations to suppliers. The quotas are typically used to balance supply, control environmental and safety impacts, and manage prices in a market that is heavily influenced by Chinese production and processing capacity.

The 2025 plan follows a period in which Chinese rare-earth production has been closely watched by manufacturers abroad as they seek more predictable input costs. While global demand for rare-earth magnets has continued to rise, competition within China and changes in the pricing of individual separated elements have affected margins for miners and processors.

Market participants have also been monitoring whether China would adjust quotas amid tighter global supply conditions and ongoing efforts to maintain an orderly market. China has previously cited the need to prevent disorderly expansion and to support stable production and investment, while the industry has looked for signals on whether restrictions would loosen or tighten.

The Ministry of Natural Resources did not immediately provide a public comment on the quota release. However, a rare-earth industry association representative said the unchanged quota suggests the government intends to maintain continuity in production management while watching downstream demand. “The quota staying at last year’s level reflects a stable policy stance,” the person said, adding that companies will continue to plan production based on allocations and guidance for compliance.

China is the world’s dominant producer of rare earths and a central supplier of separated rare-earth products used by electronics and clean energy technologies. The quota decision is expected to influence near-term operating schedules for mining firms and the output of magnet materials and other refined products.
bodies_by_headline_hash.af4a7045784cf998a66b:
Tantalum exports from the Democratic Republic of Congo have resumed at levels last seen before the country’s most recent periods of armed disruption, according to industry data reviewed by analysts.

Since early 2026, shipment volumes have returned to a steady baseline across key trading routes from central and eastern provinces, as processing capacity at regional hubs has normalized and selected buyers have reopened regular offtake arrangements. The uptick follows a gradual easing of violence and improved access to mining areas, though some sites remain intermittently affected by security conditions.

Tantalum, a metal used primarily in capacitors for electronics, is mined in the DRC alongside tin in alluvial deposits. DRC accounted for a large share of global tantalum supply in recent years, and changes in its export cadence typically ripple through electronics supply chains, with downstream firms monitoring both volumes and compliance documentation under international due-diligence rules.

Market participants said the current recovery reflects smoother logistics from mines to smelters and traders, including fewer delays at checkpoints and more predictable transport schedules. Several exporters also increased consolidated deliveries to reduce demurrage costs, a shift that has helped maintain continuity while regional inspection and documentation processes catch up after earlier disruptions.

“Export flows are now broadly back to pre-crisis patterns, but the underlying stability depends on security on the ground and the ability to document material from producing areas,” said a sourcing manager at a commodities trading firm that handles mineral shipments from the Great Lakes region. The executive added that buyers are continuing to scrutinize chain-of-custody records as authorities step up monitoring.

While output appears to have recovered in aggregate, analysts cautioned that the gains may not be uniform across all producing districts. Export figures can also reflect changes in how ore is routed—such as movements through different trading companies or processing locations—rather than only changes in mining activity.

Companies involved in tantalum processing said they have replenished inventories in anticipation of improved lead times, and some buyers have extended contracts to re-lock supply following earlier volatility. If shipment levels remain consistent over subsequent months, the DRC’s restored export run could further stabilize global availability of tantalum-bearing concentrates used in capacitor production.
bodies_by_headline_hash.63c844fe02bdd59cfeea:
Nickel markets are expected to remain oversupplied through 2027, with growing output outweighing demand, according to a forecast from Wood Mackenzie.

The research firm said the surplus would persist as new and expanded production projects come online in key producing regions while consumption growth remains moderate. It projected that supply will increase at a faster pace than end-use demand, keeping inventories under pressure and limiting prospects for a sustained balance in the physical market.

Wood Mackenzie attributed the outlook to a combination of planned capacity additions, ongoing ramp-ups of existing facilities, and higher recoveries from operations that have improved their throughput in recent years. It also pointed to continued supply availability from the stainless steel value chain, where nickel use is closely linked to broader trends in stainless demand and production schedules.

On the demand side, the firm said nickel consumption is expected to grow, but not enough to absorb the additional material. Demand momentum will depend on the pace of industrial activity, stainless steel production, and the evolving composition of nickel usage across applications, including battery-related demand. While the battery segment remains a key source of longer-term interest, Wood Mackenzie’s near-term balance assumes that substitution effects and inventory dynamics will temper how quickly new demand can tighten the market.

“Through 2027, we expect nickel to stay in surplus due to supply additions outpacing demand growth,” said a Wood Mackenzie analyst, adding that market balances would hinge on how quickly new capacity ramps and whether consumption accelerates beyond current projections.

The company said the oversupply is likely to influence pricing and market behavior, including trading patterns in the near term. With more material expected to be available, buyers may face stronger negotiating positions, while producers could be more focused on managing output levels and sales schedules to reduce the risk of prolonged low margins.

Wood Mackenzie’s forecast also suggested that any tightening could depend on disruptions or delays to planned projects, changes in operating rates at existing assets, or a faster-than-expected improvement in demand. However, absent such shocks, the firm expects the market to remain characterized by surplus conditions through the end of 2027.
bodies_by_headline_hash.86108c376898226392b6:
Lithium spodumene prices eased as Chinese converters drew down inventory and offered less stringent bids, according to market participants on Tuesday.

Benchmark prices for 5.0% Li2O spodumene concentrate fell after buyers in China were able to absorb recent supply without locking in long lead times, traders said. Several cargoes that had been contracted earlier were delivered as scheduled, reducing the immediate need for converters to secure additional material at higher levels, they added.

Converters’ activity has been supported by steady demand for lithium salts used in battery cathode production, but buying interest for additional spodumene has remained selective, traders said. With converter inventories viewed as adequate for near-term operations, spot procurement softened and sellers were compelled to lower offers to maintain sales momentum.

In addition, spot market liquidity in China has been thinner than usual as some buyers shifted attention to alternative inputs and pricing formats, sources familiar with negotiations said. Suppliers reported increased resistance to bids where impurities and freight costs were factored into final delivered prices, contributing to tighter deal flow and more price pressure on the benchmark grade.

Prices for other spodumene grades also moved, though participants said declines were more uneven. Several traders noted that demand for higher-grade material remained firm compared with lower specifications, with premiums continuing to reflect chemical conversion yields and processing costs at Chinese refineries.

One trader described the latest move as “a gradual normalization” in pricing following a period of rapid inventory buildup and frequent spot purchases. “When converters feel stocked for a few weeks, they stop chasing the front of the market and let deals clear at lower levels,” the trader said.

Market watchers said the next direction for spodumene will depend on how quickly converter inventories are drawn down and whether China’s lithium chemicals production ramps up further. They also pointed to the impact of freight rates and exchange rates on delivered concentrate pricing for importers, which can alter the effective competitiveness of offers from Australia and other suppliers.

Overall trading remained cautious, with buyers emphasizing contract flexibility and delivery timing. Traders said they expect more clarity later in the week as follow-on cargoes are priced and shipped ahead of future converter replenishment cycles.
bodies_by_headline_hash.c0c3bf995cc87b8ed0cb:
Kazakhstan has revived export permit requirements for scandium products after authorities adjusted the licensing framework, according to industry participants tracking shipments of the rare metal.

The renewed permits apply to exports of scandium and scandium-containing materials leaving the country, restoring a process that had been suspended or changed in recent months. Traders said the update is intended to bring cross-border sales under tighter monitoring and to ensure compliance with Kazakhstan’s trade and industrial regulations.

Exporters must submit documentation to the relevant government bodies before shipments can be released, several market sources said. The paperwork typically includes product classification details, quantity declarations and proof of compliance with domestic handling and transport rules, they added. Approval timelines vary, depending on the completeness of applications and the volume of consignments.

The restart comes as scandium demand remains tied to aerospace and specialty manufacturing uses, including components that benefit from scandium’s ability to improve strength and performance in certain alloys. Because scandium supply is limited globally and prices can swing sharply on changes to export availability, exporters and buyers said the permit reinstatement could affect delivery schedules.

A representative for a Kazakhstan-based trading firm said the updated permits are back in place, but that companies are working to align their contracts and shipping plans with the new documentation steps. “We are submitting applications for current and upcoming orders to avoid delays at the border,” the representative said.

Market participants said there is no immediate indication that Kazakhstan will change its overall production or stock levels, but the licensing shift may influence near-term volumes reaching international buyers. Some buyers have already asked suppliers to confirm permit status before finalizing delivery terms, sources said.

The government action also highlights Kazakhstan’s broader role in the rare earth and critical minerals supply chain, where regulatory approvals can be a key determinant of market access. Traders said they will monitor how consistently permits are issued and whether additional revisions are introduced in the coming weeks, particularly for higher-purity scandium fractions and mixed materials.
bodies_by_headline_hash.2d90d9a97ee77619b312:
Neodymium prices over the past decade have moved in sharp cycles.  
The long-term chart shows repeated drawdowns followed by short bursts of recovery.  
EV sales have risen steadily through the same period, with only minor pauses.  
That contrast highlights a widening gap between predictable demand growth and volatile supply pricing.  
When neodymium spikes, battery and magnet cost pressure can spread quickly across the value chain.  
When prices fall, it can still take time for producers to add capacity and for buyers to rebuild inventories.  
Rare earth dependency remains a key variable for EV economics.
bodies_by_headline_hash.d2f9f407de01e3d85447:
Graphite suppliers have warned that tight availability is likely to persist into the third quarter, citing limited production capacity, ongoing demand from battery and industrial customers, and slow turnaround times for some upstream processing steps.

In market updates shared with customers and trade partners, several suppliers said deliveries could remain constrained through Q3 as producers work through contracted allocations and seek to align output with demand for battery-grade material. They pointed to continued strain in the supply chain for natural and synthetic graphite, including processing and purification stages that determine whether material can meet specifications for electric vehicle and energy storage applications.

The warnings come as battery manufacturers and component makers continue to plan for steady intake of graphite concentrates and processed products. While some suppliers have increased production at specific sites, they said output additions are not expected to fully offset the broader mismatch between feedstock availability and downstream conversion capacity in the near term.

Some companies also referenced logistics and inventory dynamics as a factor. Shipment schedules for bulk material can be affected by port congestion and vessel availability, while buyers working through existing stockpiles may still need supplementary volumes if qualification cycles or grade upgrades extend beyond initial timelines, suppliers said.

“Current contract and order patterns indicate a tighter market for battery-grade and related commercial grades as we move into Q3,” one graphite supplier executive said in a statement to customers, adding that lead times for certain batches remain longer than historical averages. The executive said suppliers were continuing to prioritize committed demand while monitoring spot conditions for possible relief later in the year.

Industry analysts have previously flagged that graphite markets can swing quickly when disruptions occur upstream or when procurement shifts toward higher-grade material. In the latest supplier guidance, companies did not cite any single production incident but instead described a broadly constrained operating environment, with limited flexibility to add extra tonnage on short notice.

Suppliers said they expect negotiations on pricing and allocation to remain active during Q2, with terms likely reflecting grade specifications, delivery windows, and buyers’ reliance on qualified supply. Several firms indicated they were seeking to secure longer-term offtake arrangements and expand working inventories at processing facilities to reduce the risk of further delays.
bodies_by_headline_hash.a475bf2acd2359779012:
Rio Tinto said on Monday it would pay a dividend broadly in line with market expectations, as the miner reported results that remained supported by strong demand for key commodities.

In a trading update, the company declared an ordinary dividend of 1.50 Australian dollars per share for the period, a figure that matched a consensus estimate compiled by analysts. The dividend is to be paid in the coming weeks, subject to shareholder approval at its annual general meeting. Rio Tinto also said it would continue to review capital allocation, including potential share buybacks, depending on commodity price conditions and earnings outlook.

The announcement comes as investors focus on how major miners balance payouts against spending plans aimed at sustaining production and managing energy and emissions costs. Rio Tinto said it expects capital expenditure to remain elevated in the near term, reflecting sustaining projects and work tied to safety, infrastructure and operational reliability across its network of mines.

Operational performance during the period showed resilience, with the company pointing to production levels in its iron ore and copper businesses. Rio Tinto said its portfolio continued to be shaped by contracted sales in some markets, while pricing for other products remains linked to global benchmarks. The miner also highlighted progress on initiatives designed to improve productivity and reduce unit costs.

In its update, Rio Tinto reiterated that it has maintained a disciplined approach to costs and capital management. “Our focus remains on delivering reliable production and maintaining a strong balance between returns to shareholders and investment in the business,” the company said in a statement.

Market participants have tracked Rio Tinto’s dividend policy closely since recent years of volatility in commodity prices and changing investor expectations around capital returns. Following the latest announcement, analysts said the decision suggests the company is prioritizing a steady payout while keeping flexibility for future spending and potential adjustments to guidance.

Rio Tinto’s shares were indicated higher in early trade, reflecting the dividend alignment with consensus and the absence of major changes to its stated financial priorities.
bodies_by_headline_hash.3e547bb220e00086c546:
Platinum prices rose on Wednesday as market participants pointed to improving demand expectations for the metal used in automotive catalysts, with traders also balancing recent supply concerns against firmer physical sentiment.

Spot platinum gained in early trade after underperforming in prior sessions, supported by a pickup in inquiries for catalyst-grade material from buyers in key regions. Platinum is widely used in three-way catalysts for gasoline engines and in diesel after-treatment systems, and analysts said even modest changes in near-term consumption can move prices because the market remains sensitive to changes in production and recycling flows.

Dealers said sentiment improved as physical buyers showed greater willingness to source metal for delivery, particularly where inventories have tightened. In industrial markets, catalyst supply chains are often adjusted with lead times, and several traders noted that procurement decisions for upcoming production cycles are being reconsidered as prices stabilize.

Futures markets also reflected the shift. Platinum for delivery on the most active contract month firmed, tracking strength in related precious metals while maintaining a premium versus palladium for parts of the session. Traders said positioning remained a key factor, with some funds reducing short exposure after price declines earlier in the month. Options activity suggested interest in upside moves, though dealers cautioned that liquidity could thin quickly if macro-driven selling returns.

On the supply side, market participants continued to monitor labor and operational risks in major producing countries, as well as the pace of mine output and refining capacity. South Africa, which accounts for a significant share of global supply, has faced recurring challenges ranging from logistics disruptions to energy constraints. Any sustained disruption would tighten the balance, but traders said the latest information has not escalated materially.

“Catalyst demand is the fundamental driver we’re seeing in the short term, and it’s helping absorb near-term supply concerns,” a metals trader at a European bank said. “If physical buying continues, it should keep supporting prices even if broader risk sentiment fluctuates.”

Market watchers said the direction of platinum over the next few weeks will likely hinge on how quickly automotive inventories are replenished and whether Chinese and European industrial purchasing remains steady. They added that currency moves and interest-rate expectations could influence trading flows, while recycling volumes could further affect the tightness of available material.
bodies_by_headline_hash.4288e4cc4244c2bcb2ba:
Global supply of gallium is expected to remain broadly balanced through 2027, with available volumes likely to track demand after a period of tighter availability, according to industry forecasts cited by market participants.

Gallium, a metal used in semiconductors, solar technologies and defence-related applications such as radar systems, has faced supply constraints in recent years as production capacity has been concentrated and tied to aluminium and zinc processing. Fastmarkets and other industry watchers have been monitoring how new extraction projects and output recoveries could affect availability over the next several quarters.

The outlook for 2027 reflects a combination of steadier primary and by-product production, alongside incremental new supply from capacity expansions and contract structures that typically determine offtake timing. Several producers have been working to improve recovery rates and processing yields at facilities that produce gallium from industrial streams, which can help stabilise output even when feedstock availability varies.

Demand expectations over the same period also appear more moderate than in previous cycles, supported by continued wafer and device growth but offset by technology mix changes and inventory adjustments by downstream customers. Market participants said that while end-use demand for gallium-bearing products continues, buying patterns tend to move with order visibility and procurement policies, which can reduce the likelihood of sudden shortages.

“Based on current indicators, the market balance into 2027 looks broadly even, with no significant structural deficit apparent,” said one source familiar with the supply-demand assessment, speaking on condition of anonymity because the information was not public.

The assessment also takes into account the timing of procurement for semiconductor supply chains and the seasonal pattern of purchases by buyers that support working stock. In recent years, gallium’s pricing and availability have been influenced by both physical spot market conditions and the availability of longer-term supply contracts, which can be renegotiated when production disruptions occur.

Traders and buyers said they are watching developments including the ramp-up of new processing routes, potential regulatory changes affecting metal recovery and transport, and production stability among upstream processors. While any interruption in aluminium and zinc refining, or delays in gallium recovery operations, could tighten availability, the general view is that supply additions planned up to 2027 should keep the market within a manageable range.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ad73802fe78ed96ecffc:
Indium prices rose to a two-year high as demand from the semiconductor sector strengthened and buyers competed for supplies of the scarce metal, according to market participants and trading data.

In early trading, indium was quoted at levels not seen since early 2024, supported by tighter availability and firmer pricing for related feedstock used in indium tin oxide and other electronic materials. Traders said the latest move reflected a pickup in end-market orders rather than short-covering, with manufacturers seeking materials ahead of expected production schedules.

Indium is used primarily in flat-panel displays, touchscreens and photovoltaic systems, but its use in semiconductor manufacturing has gained attention as producers expand process capacity. Several suppliers have also reported that refining runs remain constrained by limited concentrate availability and by contractual offtake arrangements, which have reduced spot supply for spot purchases.

“Semiconductor-linked demand has been pulling on the physical market, and lead times are still not easing,” said one metals trader familiar with pricing negotiations, adding that the rise had been amplified by thin liquidity in cash markets. The trader did not provide further details.

Analysts said the move also aligns with broader trends in electronic materials, where stronger chip-related production has translated into higher buying interest for specialty metals. While indium is not a large component of most finished chips, it is critical for certain materials and coatings used in manufacturing steps and in components tied to electronic devices.

In addition to demand, the price increase reflects lingering supply-side pressures. Some producers have maintained cautious output levels, citing the cost of refining and the need to balance long-term contracts with spot sales. Recycling of indium from manufacturing waste remains an important source, but traders said recycling volumes have not been sufficient to offset shortfalls in refined supply.

Market watchers said they would monitor whether the higher prices prompt additional supply, including more spot shipments from refiners and increased recycling activity, which could influence the next leg of the market. For now, they said, the balance of physical availability and semiconductor-driven demand continues to support elevated quotations.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ebfedaa960fbbe587920:
Uranium enrichment capacity will remain constrained through at least 2028, limiting the pace at which new supplies can be produced, according to industry and supply-chain analyses reviewed by Reuters.

The assessment points to a mismatch between rising demand expectations and the slower addition or ramp-up of enrichment services at major facilities. While some projects have moved forward, the timetable for new centrifuge capacity and the availability of required components and feedstock are expected to keep usable capacity tight, particularly during periods when multiple customer programs overlap.

Enrichment, a key step in producing reactor fuel, is typically supplied under long-term contracts that allocate centrifuge operating time. Several of the world’s enrichment facilities have been operating at varying levels as they balance maintenance schedules and upgrades, a dynamic that can delay the delivery of additional separative work units—the measure used to price enrichment services.

A senior executive at an enrichment-related service provider said the constraint is not only about installed equipment but also about “getting the full system to stable output,” including commissioning, performance validation and supply-chain lead times for parts and services. “Even when expansion is approved, the usable capacity shows up only after the enrichment trains are fully qualified,” the executive said.

The report also highlights that enrichment demand is being shaped by a growing number of fuel-cycle commitments and by inventory management strategies used by utilities. Utilities and fuel fabricators may try to place orders in advance to secure feed arrangements, but those efforts can be limited if enrichment providers have not yet reached the operating levels required to fulfill additional contracts.

Geopolitical and regulatory factors continue to influence how enrichment services are delivered. Restrictions and compliance requirements can affect the scheduling of deliveries and the ability to redirect feed through specific stages of the fuel cycle. In addition, customers seeking to meet long-term reactor fuel needs must account for potential lead times between contract placement, feed conversion, enrichment processing and fabrication.

The analyses suggest that any additional easing of the constraint will likely depend on the completion of upgrade programs at operating plants and on the timing of new centrifuge installations. In the absence of earlier-than-planned commissioning milestones, the supply squeeze through 2028 is expected to keep enrichment pricing supported and maintain bargaining pressure on contract terms, according to market participants.
bodies_by_headline_hash.24eccea261d21b85a20e:
The molybdenum market showed resilience after a production disruption in China, with traders and analysts saying supply concerns were contained and prices held steady.

The outage, affecting processing capacity in parts of China’s molybdenum supply chain, prompted some near-term caution among buyers, particularly for high-grade material used in steelmaking and alloy production. However, market participants said inventories and alternative sourcing routes limited the impact on contracted supply, reducing the likelihood of immediate price shocks.

In spot dealings, prices for molybdenum concentrates and related products were largely unchanged over the past week, according to trade sources. Several buyers said they had been able to adjust procurement schedules without resorting to larger premiums, reflecting the current balance between mill demand and available stockpiles in key trading regions.

A key factor, analysts said, was that the outage did not fully interrupt upstream mining output, but rather constrained downstream processing and refining volumes. That distinction mattered for the timing of any supply gap, since molybdenum products used in industrial applications typically move through multiple stages before reaching end users. As a result, any tighter supply would be expected first in specific grades or product forms, rather than across the entire market.

“We expect the disruption to be manageable because it is not a total shutdown of Chinese supply,” one Asia-based metals trader said. “The market is watching whether processing restarts quickly, but for now the physical flows look steady.”

Chinese producers have sought to maintain output by reallocating feedstock and adjusting maintenance schedules, traders said. Some buyers also reported that offers for certain intermediates were slightly more firm, while pricing for finished material remained stable, suggesting localized rather than systemic pressure.

Industry demand for molybdenum is closely linked to steel production levels, particularly where alloy steels are used in construction, energy equipment and automotive components. With global mills operating at relatively normal rates, traders said the market’s ability to absorb temporary disruptions depended on near-term purchasing patterns and the replenishment cycle of inventories.

While the outage kept attention on China’s processing capacity, participants said broader market signals—such as credit terms, freight costs and contract rollover activity—were still the main drivers for sentiment. They indicated price direction would likely hinge on the duration of the disruption and any follow-on effects on product availability in the following loading weeks.
bodies_by_headline_hash.277b957b17a8aba096f1:
Tin inventories monitored on the London Metal Exchange remained at multi-decade lows on Monday, underscoring tight supplies and continued pressure on warehouse stocks.

According to LME data compiled by market participants, registered tin stocks in London were unchanged on the day and stayed near the lowest levels seen in decades. The inventories have trended down over recent months as demand for physical metal has outpaced warrant arrivals, leaving fewer units available for immediate delivery against exchange contracts.

The reduction in stored metal has coincided with relatively limited inflows into the LME system, with deliveries largely dependent on producer shipments and arbitrage flows from other trading hubs. Where tonnage has arrived, it has often been quickly withdrawn for settlement needs, limiting the buildup of stocks that normally accompanies seasonal demand.

Traders said the tight inventory situation has contributed to a persistent bid for near-term metal, with attention focused on any changes in supply schedules from producing countries. While LME stocks provide a snapshot of metal available for settlement through the exchange, analysts said they also serve as a benchmark for broader market sentiment among buyers seeking prompt availability.

In addition to physical tightness, the structure of the tin forward curve has reflected the supply backdrop. When near-dated prices trade firm relative to further maturities, it can indicate that market participants are pricing in limited near-term availability even as longer-dated contracts look to future production. Any shift in that structure can be influenced by changes in stock levels, freight conditions, and producer offtake patterns.

One London-based metals broker said that “the market is still watching the registered numbers closely because they remain the clearest indicator of how much tin is sitting ready for warrant-based trading.” The broker added that the next potential catalyst would be the scale and timing of incoming tonnage that can determine whether inventories stabilize or continue to decline.

Despite the low stock levels, LME inventory data can move quickly on a small number of warrant movements. Market watchers said they will be monitoring weekly deliveries and cancellations, alongside any signals from smelters and logistics channels, for indications that inventory levels could change in the coming sessions.
bodies_by_headline_hash.8887a941624faef48e2e:
Rare-earth prices are likely to move back toward more normal levels by the end of the year as supply expands and demand expectations stabilize, industry participants said, pointing to easing disruptions and slower inventory drawdowns after a period of volatility.

The rebound in market sentiment follows a sequence of disruptions that had tightened availability of several rare-earths used in magnets and electronics. Those include delays in refining capacity, shipping constraints affecting material flows from China and elsewhere, and periodic buying spikes tied to customer contract renegotiations.

One factor supporting a normalization trajectory is the gradual increase in separation and refining output outside the narrowest parts of the rare-earth supply chain. Several projects that had been delayed in previous quarters are now approaching steady production, reducing the need for customers to source on short notice and bid up spot volumes. In parallel, exporters and processors have increased reliance on more diversified feedstock arrangements, which has helped lower the risk of sudden shortages for specific rare-earth elements.

Demand-side expectations are also shifting. While long-term use in electric vehicles, wind turbines and industrial motors remains firm, near-term procurement has become more measured as some buyers completed earlier stocking plans. Companies have been prioritizing contracted volumes over spot purchases, which tends to dampen price swings when the market is no longer reacting to urgent availability constraints.

Market normalization is also being supported by improved visibility on inventories and by changes in how rare-earth products are traded and delivered. In recent months, more material has been routed through standard logistics channels rather than emergency shipments, lowering cost pressures that had fed into spot pricing. Traders said that, as deliveries catch up with orders, price levels typically reset toward contract benchmarks.

“By the end of the year, we expect a broader easing in the most strained grades as supply stabilizes and downstream buyers rebalance their inventories,” said a metals analyst at a commodity research firm, who cited incremental capacity ramp-ups and reduced spot competition.

Still, participants cautioned that normalization would likely be uneven across individual rare-earth elements. Some grades, particularly those with tighter specific production pathways, may take longer to rebalance. Any renewed disruptions—such as regulatory changes, disruptions to power-intensive operations, or fresh logistical bottlenecks—could also delay the timing of price stabilization.
bodies_by_headline_hash.3619b741a5f08f50d1bd:
Mountain Pass, the United States’ major rare-earth mining and processing operation in California, emerged from bankruptcy after a turnaround that combined restructuring, new supply arrangements and government-backed financing, according to people familiar with the company’s plans and court filings reviewed by Bloomberg.

The company, which produces rare-earth materials used in magnets for electric vehicles, wind turbines and military systems, entered bankruptcy protection in 2022 amid falling commodity prices and rising costs, including higher energy and hauling expenses. During the proceedings, Mountain Pass’ assets were kept operating in a limited capacity while lenders and other stakeholders negotiated a path to restore production and settle claims.

In subsequent months, the company shifted focus toward stabilizing cash flow and securing offtake agreements that would help underwrite working capital needs. Several contracts with processors and downstream buyers were designed to reduce volatility in rare-earth pricing and volumes, people familiar with the negotiations said. The company also worked to align its processing strategy with demand for specific rare-earth outputs, including neodymium and praseodymium compounds used in high-performance permanent magnets.

A central element of the recovery was financing structured around capital spending and production milestones. Court-approved funding packages included debtor-in-possession credit and later arrangements intended to support ongoing operations and environmental and maintenance work. Federal and state involvement also played a role, with programs that target domestic rare-earth supply chains supporting portions of the restructuring effort, the people said.

Mountain Pass’ recovery was not limited to financing. The company reported that it improved procurement and logistics, reduced exposure to certain suppliers, and increased operational uptime as repairs and upgrades were completed. It also sought to bring additional processing capacity online, including improvements intended to lower conversion costs for intermediate rare-earth products.

By the time the bankruptcy process concluded, stakeholders had agreed to a plan that rebalanced the company’s capital structure and placed it on what management described as a sustainable production footing. “The focus was on getting back to consistent production, securing customer demand and putting the balance sheet on a path to stability,” one of the people familiar with the restructuring said.

Since exiting bankruptcy, Mountain Pass has continued work to sustain output and expand longer-term arrangements with customers, while monitoring rare-earth market conditions. The company’s turnaround has been viewed by some suppliers and regulators as a test case for rebuilding critical-mineral capacity in the U.S. amid shifting demand for magnet-ready materials.
bodies_by_headline_hash.2708389a6fb902854339:
Rare-earth magnets are specialized magnets used widely in electronics and clean-energy technologies, and their performance depends on the way rare-earth elements are processed and engineered, industry experts say.

At the core of most of these magnets are compounds based on neodymium and praseodymium, or on samarium and cobalt. When manufactured, the materials are formed into blocks or thin discs and then magnetized, producing a strong magnetic field relative to their size. That high “energy product” is what makes them valuable in motors, generators, microphones, and medical imaging devices, where weight and efficiency are critical.

Unlike common iron-based magnets, rare-earth magnets are made from materials that are brittle and typically require sintering, machining and careful coatings to prevent corrosion. The manufacturing process also involves refining rare-earth oxides into metals and then combining them with iron and boron in precise proportions for neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) magnets, or with cobalt for samarium-cobalt (SmCo) versions. Slight variations in alloy composition, grain size and manufacturing conditions can significantly affect magnetic strength and temperature stability.

In the clean-energy sector, rare-earth magnets are used in permanent magnet motors, including those in electric vehicles and wind turbines. In many wind designs, the magnets help generate electricity at lower rotational speeds and can improve system efficiency. In electric vehicles, they can contribute to higher power density, allowing motors to be smaller for a given output. The magnets are also incorporated in generators and actuators used in robotics and industrial automation.

Performance is shaped by operating conditions. NdFeB magnets can be sensitive to heat and can partially lose magnetization if exposed to temperatures beyond design limits. SmCo magnets generally tolerate higher temperatures and can resist demagnetization better, though they are often more expensive. Magnet grades are selected based on the required field strength and expected operating temperature range.

Supply chains remain a key factor in how the technology is deployed. Rare-earth elements are mined and separated through chemical processing steps that concentrate individual elements, and downstream manufacturers then produce magnets through alloying, sintering and finishing. “The strength comes from both the chemistry and the microstructure you lock in during manufacturing,” said one materials engineer familiar with magnet production, noting that consistent quality requires tight control at each stage.

Efforts to reduce reliance on rare-earth magnets include developing alternative magnet chemistries, increasing recycling of scrap magnets, and designing equipment that uses less material. Still, for many high-efficiency applications, rare-earth magnets remain difficult to replace without trade-offs in performance, size or cost.
bodies_by_headline_hash.458dd7c52c0561c29faa:
A new episode of a monthly metals roundtable podcast focusing on commodity markets is set to be released, bringing industry participants together to review recent price moves and assess near-term developments.

The discussion, scheduled as part of the routine programming offered by Fastmarkets, will cover market conditions across multiple metals categories, with attention on supply patterns, trading liquidity and key drivers affecting demand. Participants are expected to outline how recent manufacturing and procurement trends are playing out in spot and contract markets, and how shifts in logistics and lead times could influence deliveries in the coming weeks.

In the roundtable, speakers are expected to revisit the latest developments in rare earths and related materials, where prices and availability can be influenced by production planning, export controls and ongoing changes in downstream processing capacity. The episode is also expected to address how investors and end users are reacting to cost pressures, including adjustments to buying schedules and inventory strategies.

Beyond rare earths, the podcast segment is expected to touch on broader metals market themes, including how energy costs and freight rates are affecting input prices, and whether exchange and over-the-counter flows are supporting current levels. Speakers are set to discuss the implications of policy decisions and regulatory updates in major producing regions, as well as the prospects for project timelines and mine output.

One participant, according to the event preview, will highlight how “pricing signals continue to reflect both physical market tightness and expectations for future supply,” noting that contract negotiations and spot purchases remain sensitive to incremental changes in availability and conversion capacity.

The podcast episode will be distributed through the publisher’s podcast channel and corresponding online platforms. It is designed to provide a consistent monthly forum for market participants to summarize developments and share perspectives on how conditions are evolving across the metals complex, including the materials most closely tied to industrial demand chains.
bodies_by_headline_hash.391e06470e460f543aec:
Investors pulled down holdings in rare-earth exchange-traded funds to their lowest level in six months, according to data on Thursday, as demand for the sector cooled and broader market risk sentiment improved.

The outflows reduced net assets held in the category’s main listed products, with weekly transactions turning negative after several sessions of modest inflows earlier in the year. Analysts said the shift reflected a combination of lighter allocation to high-volatility commodities and investors taking profits following a recent run-up in related industrial and mining themes.

The decline comes as rare earths, which are used in permanent magnets for electric vehicles, wind turbines and consumer electronics, remain sensitive to supply-chain developments and industrial demand signals. Prices for some concentrates and magnet-related inputs have fluctuated in recent months, while plans for new processing capacity in key producing regions have continued to progress at varying speeds.

Inflows had been supported earlier by expectations that clean-energy spending would sustain long-term demand, but attention has also turned to near-term manufacturing indicators. Fund flows tend to react quickly to changes in forward demand views, and market participants said the latest numbers suggest investors are waiting for clearer catalysts on end-use demand and on the economics of downstream projects.

“One reason we saw selling is that many investors treat rare-earth exposure as a tactical allocation rather than a core holding,” said a spokesperson at a commodities-focused asset manager, adding that positioning has been adjusted as momentum in the space eased.

Despite the pullback, some investors maintained exposure through existing ETF holdings rather than exiting entirely. Trading volumes remained active, and the funds’ holdings mix continued to track underlying baskets tied to companies involved in mining, refining and magnet production. Market observers said such products can still serve as a liquid entry point for investors who want concentrated exposure to a complex supply chain without buying individual equities or physical materials.

The six-month low in flows highlights the uneven appetite for commodities-linked themes as investors weigh macro factors and the timing of industrial investment. Rare-earth prices and related equities will likely remain a focus for traders looking for confirmation that demand growth is translating into sustained orders for magnet and rare-earth processing capacity.
bodies_by_headline_hash.1aa65bedb454a4d62c54:
Policy makers and industry leaders are urging governments to diversify supply chains for critical minerals used in batteries, power grids and advanced manufacturing, citing heightened geopolitical risk and concentrated processing capacity, according to a policy commentary published on Monday.

The piece argues that many mineral supply routes remain overly dependent on a small number of countries for extraction, refining and component production. It points to bottlenecks where a handful of jurisdictions control the intermediate processing steps that determine whether raw materials can be turned into usable inputs for downstream manufacturers.

The commentary says the concentration problem has been reinforced by a combination of factors, including export restrictions, trade disputes, enforcement of environmental and labor rules, and periodic disruptions to shipping and production. It notes that even when new mines come online, the timeline for building processing capacity and refining capacity can extend far beyond the lifespan of initial investment cycles, leaving manufacturers exposed to sudden price swings and shortages.

It also highlights the role of “midstream” processing—refining, chemical conversion and material production—as a key vulnerability. Diversification, the author argues, should therefore focus not only on securing additional sources of ore, but also on expanding and qualifying alternative refining and processing pathways in multiple regions.

The commentary recommends a package of measures aimed at reducing single-point failures. These include streamlined permitting for new processing facilities, targeted public funding or loan guarantees for early-stage projects, and support for long-term offtake agreements that help investors finance infrastructure. It also calls for greater transparency in reporting the provenance of minerals and for international cooperation on standards for environmental safeguards and responsible sourcing.

In an interview accompanying the publication, one analyst said diversification should be treated as an industrial policy challenge rather than solely a trading strategy. “If you only diversify where the ore is mined, but processing remains concentrated, manufacturers still face the same bottleneck,” the analyst said. “Building multiple lanes for conversion and refining is what changes the risk profile.”

The commentary further suggests that governments should coordinate inventories and procurement planning across sectors that compete for the same materials, such as batteries and renewable-energy equipment. It argues that stable demand signals, coupled with clear rules for recycling and recovery, can gradually reduce reliance on high-risk supply chains while maintaining steady input flows for manufacturers.
bodies_by_headline_hash.7923a9a966663c19a923:
Rare-earth prices were mixed in the latest weekly trading wrap, with praseodymium flat while terbium held steady across key contract indicators, according to market participants tracking the sector.

Praseodymium prices were reported unchanged over the week as buyers and sellers maintained price positions amid a narrow balance between supply availability and demand interest. Several traders said near-term buying activity remained selective, with market sentiment influenced by the timing of restocking cycles and the availability of material from contracted channels.

Terbium, used in applications including magnets and specialty electronics, was assessed as steady during the week. Desk reports indicated that spot offers and deal discussions were largely contained within established ranges, limiting day-to-day movement. One factor cited by traders was the continued alignment between product availability and offtake plans, which reduced pressure for either upward repricing or discounting.

Analysts said the broader market remained watchful of downstream demand signals, particularly for rare-earth magnet supply chains tied to electric motors and other industrial equipment. Although activity in end-use markets has fluctuated, participants noted that pricing for individual elements often changes independently based on availability and the negotiating positions of buyers and producers.

In the week’s assessments, most movement came from negotiation dynamics rather than broad market repricing. Several participants pointed to ongoing efforts to secure quantities for the coming months, with buyers seeking clarity on delivery schedules and grades while sellers focused on maintaining terms consistent with recent benchmarks.

Market participants also highlighted the importance of non-price factors, including lead times, logistics and payment structures, which can influence whether deals are executed at quoted levels. Even where price indices showed little change, they said liquidity and the ability to source specific forms of material could determine transaction outcomes.

As trading progressed, participants said they would continue monitoring contract activity for signs of whether stable levels for praseodymium and terbium would extend into the next weekly cycle. Expectations for further movement were tied to the pace of procurement by magnet and manufacturing customers and the availability of material from established supply arrangements.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ee31e8b743b9d719633f:
MP Materials said on Thursday it expects second-quarter rare-earth oxide production to be roughly in line with the prior quarter as demand from manufacturers of permanent magnets remains steady, according to updated guidance.

The U.S.-listed company, which mines rare earths at the Mountain Pass site in California, forecast Q2 total rare-earth oxide production of between 12,700 and 13,000 metric tons. That range would put output near the level it reported for the first quarter, when production was affected by operational adjustments and variability in feedstock processing.

MP Materials also guided Q2 separated and other rare-earth product production, a figure that reflects additional processing beyond mining and leaching. The company expects separated rare-earth oxide to fall within a band of 12,000 to 12,400 metric tons, while offering more granular expectations for certain high-value products used in traction motors and industrial applications.

In its update, MP Materials reiterated that it continues to focus on throughput and recovery rates at Mountain Pass, alongside efforts to manage inventory levels to support customer commitments. The company said its production profile is tied to the ramp-up and optimization of processing steps, including the conversion of intermediate materials into separated oxides at its processing facilities.

Looking beyond volumes, MP Materials said it expects Q2 cash costs to remain consistent with previously communicated levels, with costs influenced by energy prices, reagent consumption and maintenance activities at the plant. The company also referenced normal working-capital movements that can affect quarterly results, including changes in inventory and accounts receivable tied to customer shipments.

“We are guiding to output that is broadly consistent with the first quarter, reflecting continued execution at Mountain Pass and stable conditions in our supply chain,” MP Materials Chief Executive Matthew Morley said in the statement.

MP Materials’ rare-earth products are used in magnets that support electric vehicles, wind power and a range of industrial equipment. The company has previously described a multi-year effort to expand its ability to separate rare earths, a segment that can command a premium because it reduces reliance on overseas processing.

The company’s guidance comes as global rare-earth supply chains face continued geopolitical and industrial pressures, with manufacturers seeking more domestic sources of both mined and refined materials. MP Materials is expected to provide additional detail on its second-quarter outlook when it reports results later in the quarter.
bodies_by_headline_hash.947fc47fc960eba5d282:
Rare-earth shipments from Malaysia have resumed on a routine schedule after a brief disruption, industry sources said, easing short-term supply concerns for buyers of separated and mixed rare-earth materials.

Carriers and logistics providers involved in cross-border movements confirmed that container departures linked to Malaysia’s rare-earth refining and processing activities returned to normal frequency in early March. Several customers that had adjusted inventory plans during the delay said they received updated loading notices and transport documents consistent with prior weeks’ timetables.

The restart follows a period in which shipments were constrained by operational and administrative factors that affected throughput at processing facilities and coordination with downstream exporters. Sources familiar with the matter said the interruption was not linked to changes in export licensing and that customs clearance processes were largely functioning, though some consignments were held longer than usual.

Malaysia remains one of the more important hubs in Southeast Asia for the processing of rare-earth products used in magnets, catalysts and other industrial applications. Buyers typically rely on predictable deliveries because separated rare-earth compounds and rare-earth intermediate products are produced in batches and require continuity to support customer manufacturing schedules.

One export agent said routine departures resumed without a return to the backlog seen earlier in the quarter. “We have been moving shipments on the agreed calendar again, and paperwork clearance is keeping pace,” the agent said. The agent added that some orders were prioritised to meet longer-standing commitments, while newer purchase orders were slotted into remaining capacity.

Market participants have been watching shipment patterns across rare-earth supply chains as refiners manage feedstock availability and processing capacity amid pricing volatility and shifting demand tied to electric vehicles, wind power and electronics. Any delay in rare-earth material movements can affect downstream magnet production and the availability of specific grades used for high-performance applications.

Trade flows from Malaysia are also tracked for their role in balancing regional supply. In recent months, buyers have sought diversification across producing and processing regions to reduce reliance on single-origin routes and to mitigate logistics risks. Analysts said that a return to routine schedules can support more stable procurement and reduce the likelihood of short-term price dislocations tied to delivery timing.

For now, sources said the volume of shipments during the renewed period is in line with planned dispatches, with more detailed monthly figures expected in subsequent trade reporting.
bodies_by_headline_hash.c6d8f15a30d93679a107:
Rare-earth prices have kept tracking the 30-day moving average rather than breaking away sharply.  
That steadying effect is showing up across the most watched compounds, suggesting demand is adjusting more gradually than supply.  
The chart implies short-term spikes are getting smoothed, not ignored, as volatility clusters and then fades.
