It also outlined plans to expand production of high-value rare-earth compounds and materials, which it said are designed to improve margins as buyers shift toward more refined inputs for permanent magnet manufacturing.

In a question-and-answer session following the presentations, the chief executive said the company’s updated targets reflect “the latest execution cadence” for its projects and continued efforts to de-risk ramp-up timelines. The executive added that the firm is working to stabilize supply contracts and strengthen customer qualification for magnet-related products.

The investor day comes as the sector faces a challenging mix of policy developments, supply chain constraints and shifting customer procurement behavior. Governments in the United States, Europe and Asia have continued to weigh measures aimed at improving rare-earth security, while buyers have sought longer-term supply visibility amid periodic price swings.

The company did not announce new financing plans at the event, focusing instead on performance indicators and operational benchmarks that it said will be used to measure progress toward its long-term objectives. Analysts were expected to assess whether the restated targets imply higher or lower capital requirements and how quickly the firm expects incremental capacity to translate into revenue.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ddbad97294b773014d2d: A new scholarship fund targeting rare-earth metallurgy students was launched on 2026-03-12, with initial financing earmarked for academic years 2026–2027 through 2028–2029. The program will provide 60 awards in its first cycle, covering tuition and an annual stipend set at a flat rate of 12,000 euros for eligible full-time undergraduates and 18,000 euros for graduate recipients. Preference will be given to applicants studying extractive metallurgy, magnetic materials, and hydrometallurgy, with a minimum 3.2 grade-point average requirement for continuing scholars and a baseline of 70% for new entrants. Partners include research institutes and two equipment manufacturers, which will contribute laboratory placements and summer placements for up to 120 students. The fund will also sponsor 18 month-long internships starting in July 2026, with placements distributed across separation and refining facilities in three regions. Applications will open 2026-04-01 and close 2026-06-15, with scholarship decisions scheduled by 2026-07-20.
bodies_by_headline_hash.3d101b91245dde795e54:
A rare-earth trade mission participated in a Tokyo summit focused on critical minerals this week.  
The gathering brought government officials and industry executives together to discuss supply security for materials used in magnets, batteries, and defense technologies.  
Delegations highlighted risks from concentrated processing capacity and tightening export controls.  
Participants exchanged views on standards for responsible sourcing and traceability across the value chain.  
Organizers said the talks will feed into new frameworks aimed at reducing bottlenecks from mining to refining.  
The mission also promoted commercial deals and partnerships to expand capacity outside a small number of production centers.  
No details of specific agreements were disclosed during the event.
bodies_by_headline_hash.f381bd298a2e28d85d10:
A panel at a rare-earths conference on Thursday focused on the economics of recycling, as industry representatives weighed whether recovery of valuable metals from end-of-life products can compete with new mining.

The session brought together executives from recycling firms, component makers and research institutions to discuss cost drivers for collecting scrap, processing mixed materials and producing battery-grade or magnet-ready outputs. Speakers said recycling economics depend heavily on feedstock availability, commodity price swings and the ability to separate rare-earth elements from complex alloys and consumer waste streams.

One panelist said the economics improved when recycling systems targeted specific product categories, such as permanent magnets used in electric motors and generators, rather than relying on mixed streams from electronics. “The economics of recycling start with sorting,” said an industry participant during the discussion, noting that concentrating rare-earth-containing parts before treatment can reduce processing losses and lower refining costs. The panel also highlighted the role of standardized product designs that make disassembly and material recovery easier.

Participants described current technologies used to recover rare earths, including hydrometallurgical processes that dissolve and separate metals using chemical reagents, and emerging approaches that aim to cut energy use and reduce waste. They said the choice of method is influenced by the form of the rare-earth material in the original product, as well as contamination levels. Several speakers pointed to ongoing pilot projects that test different separation techniques for magnets and catalysts, while others described efforts to scale up throughput without increasing reagent consumption.

The panel addressed policy and market conditions that affect investment decisions, including long-term offtake agreements for recycled materials and government support for recycling infrastructure. Speakers also discussed how producers and recyclers are seeking clearer pathways to certify recycled rare earths for use in manufacturing, particularly in sectors tied to clean-energy equipment. They said that without consistent product specifications and demand, recycling capacity expansion could be limited.

Organizers said the conference is part of broader efforts to improve supply security for rare-earth elements, which are used in wind turbines, electric vehicles and telecommunications equipment. The panel concluded with calls for closer coordination between waste collection networks, recyclers and manufacturers, alongside continued research to lower processing costs and increase recovery rates.
bodies_by_headline_hash.8a11744d455222e7be33: A rare-earth producer has renewed a long-term offtake agreement with an Asian magnet maker, extending supplies of neodymium and praseodymium oxides that are used in electric motors for vehicles and industrial equipment. The new contract covers incremental volumes for delivery starting in 2026 and running through multiple quarters, according to industry filings and counterpart confirmations reviewed in recent days. Pricing is linked to a basket of spot rare-earth benchmarks and periodic adjustments for freight and conversion costs, the terms indicate. The renewal follows prior shipments that were delivered under the earlier arrangement, which had been scheduled to expire in late 2025. The magnet maker said the update improves procurement certainty for sourcing without relying on short-notice spot buys during peak demand periods. The producer did not disclose overall contract value, but the arrangement is expected to support planned processing utilization at its refining operations during 2026 as supply constraints in heavy rare-earths ease only gradually.
bodies_by_headline_hash.fe7add89b27227f22d4a:
A rare-earth lobby group has published a set of policy recommendations aimed at strengthening supply chains and reducing strategic dependence.  
The group argues that governments should diversify mining and processing outside a small number of producing countries.  
It calls for faster permitting for new and expanded processing facilities, along with support for recycling technologies that recover rare earths from magnets and electronics.  
The recommendations also urge clearer procurement rules to encourage domestic demand for permanent magnets used in wind, EV motors, and defense systems.  
Industrial subsidies and risk-sharing mechanisms are highlighted to attract investment in midstream refining capacity.  
The group says the proposals are designed to improve resilience ahead of growing global demand.
bodies_by_headline_hash.af123cd9e574cfd08575:
A rare-earth price chart tracking the 90-day moving average showed mixed trends across key elements, according to an update published by a monitoring channel on Saturday.

The chart, shared via Rare Earth Watch on Telegram, aggregates recent trading signals and smooths them by using a 90-day moving average, a method intended to reduce short-term volatility. The update does not identify specific buyers or sellers, but it summarizes how benchmark prices for several widely watched rare earths have shifted over the past quarter.

Among the elements shown, neodymium and praseodymium—often grouped as NdPr due to their shared use in magnets—registered changes in their averaged values compared with the prior period displayed on the chart. Dysprosium and terbium, both used to enhance high-temperature performance in permanent magnets, also moved on the chart, reflecting separate pricing pressure rather than tracking exactly in tandem with lighter rare earths.

The 90-day average lines indicated that some of the most liquid inputs remained range-bound, while others edged higher or lower within narrower bands. The chart format highlights directionality by comparing the smoothed series to recent levels, with the latest point marking the newest day included in the rolling calculation.

The update comes as global demand continues to hinge on applications in electric vehicles, wind turbines and industrial motors, where magnet supply chains are central. Rare-earth pricing has been sensitive to changes in export policy, inventory management and capacity developments, and analysts often use longer averaging windows to interpret underlying demand and supply shifts.

“Moving averages help show the bigger trend rather than reacting to one-off market prints,” a representative from Rare Earth Watch wrote in the accompanying post, referring to the chart’s use of a 90-day smoothing window. The channel did not provide additional context on the underlying drivers of the changes shown on the chart.

The monitoring feed is aimed at tracking price movements for rare earths and does not claim to represent official benchmark rates. Figures on the chart are based on the data sources and calculations used by the channel, and they may differ from figures maintained by exchanges or separate pricing services.
bodies_by_headline_hash.b2feb2d2478dba634ca8: A monitoring report published on March 15, 2026, said distribution restructuring in dysprosium-rare earth (DR) supply chains showed congestion patterns consistent with a typical spread. The analysis cites abnormal pacing across multiple shipment corridors during the first half of March, with lane-level dwell times rising from a baseline of under 6 days to ranges of 7–10 days in several nodes. It attributed the bottleneck to staggered export clearances and limited intermediate storage capacity rather than a sudden supply disruption, noting that arrival variance remained within historical bounds. The report found that order batching increased by an estimated 15–20% between March 6 and March 12, while reported lead-time volatility narrowed after March 10. Prices for targeted DR-linked inputs were described as moving in line with prior seasonal demand, without the step changes seen in prior stress events. Observers said the pattern suggests normal network “spread” dynamics rather than an acute systemic shortage.
bodies_by_headline_hash.f0791a6381ffc067f559:
LME rare-earth warehouse stocks stayed steady in the latest volume update.  
Tracked material levels showed no significant drawdowns or inflows across monitored lots.  
The result points to stable short-term supply for market participants watching logistics rather than production.  
Traders typically treat warehouse movement as a near-term signal for pricing pressure, especially when demand expectations swing.  
With levels holding, sentiment appears cautious, with buyers waiting for clearer catalysts in manufacturing restocking.  
Analysts will likely focus next on any changes tied to export scheduling, freight disruptions, or new offtake commitments from processing hubs.
bodies_by_headline_hash.866a7a423114aff19547:
A social media post circulating on Telegram on March 15 included what users described as routine commentary on the rare-earth market, with no immediate signs of a policy change or a new supply disruption, according to the account that shared it.

The post, published by a channel named “Rare Earth Watch,” said it was “market commentary” and referenced recent price movement and trading sentiment for select rare-earth oxides used in magnets and clean-energy components. It did not announce any new government measures or company actions. It also did not cite fresh order data or new shipment volumes, focusing instead on perceived demand trends and the current tone of buyers and sellers.

In the message, the channel highlighted that market participants continued to monitor bargaining positions from major suppliers and potential adjustments to inventories, a theme that has appeared in prior discussions in the rare-earth sector. It also pointed to the effect of financing conditions and long-term customer commitments on near-term buying patterns, according to the text of the post.

Analysts and market participants often differentiate between general price talk and verifiable developments such as export licensing changes, contracted shipments, or updates to downstream production. In this case, the Telegram message framed its content as commentary and did not reference any specific transactions or policy announcements that could be independently tied to a change in availability or cost.

One line attributed in the post to “traders” said that near-term price expectations were being set more by sentiment than by documented supply changes. The account also urged readers to track additional indicators, including upstream processing capacity utilization and downstream orders, for confirmation of any direction in the market.

The rare-earth supply chain remains closely watched because the materials are used in permanent magnets for electric vehicles, wind turbines and industrial motors. Prices for particular rare-earth products can move quickly based on expectations for demand, producer output, and the timing of procurement for magnet makers and refiners. Any new government action or confirmed shipping disruption would typically be reported with more concrete details than were contained in the March 15 post, traders said in general terms.

At the time of publication, the Telegram channel’s post was being shared as a brief market update rather than as breaking news, and it did not immediately trigger widely reported changes in policy or logistics, according to traders following the sector.
bodies_by_headline_hash.2e468ce1f6ff080e9fac: Pricing for key rare-earth inputs showed minimal month-on-month movement in the latest market check dated March 15, 2026, according to data compiled by Rare Earth Watch. Across the basket, the month-on-month pricing delta was described as near flat, with changes generally within a narrow band of single-digit percentage points between February and March. The report said baseline levels remained largely unchanged for NdPr metal and related oxide grades, despite continued spot buying from midstream processors. Praseodymium, neodymium, and dysprosium-linked product streams were also reported to hold steady, with only isolated adjustments noted by region rather than across the full supply chain. Traders attributed the lack of volatility to balanced near-term availability and muted procurement cycles after February restocking. The next pricing update is scheduled within the next reporting cycle, with attention focused on any shift in shipping lead times and contract repricing that could widen the month-on-month delta beyond the current minimal range.
bodies_by_headline_hash.57a61e31901a6380deb7:
Lithium spot prices fell about 6% after Chinese inventories increased.  
Traders pointed to slower downstream buying as buyers waited for clearer demand signals.  
Wholesalers in China reportedly added material to buffer stock, easing near-term tightness.  
That shift reduced bargaining power for sellers and pressured offers across benchmark grades.  
Market participants said the move is concentrated in short-dated contracts rather than longer term pricing.  
Attention is now turning to whether inventory accumulation continues into late March.  
Producers are watching for renewed offtake from battery makers and refiners.  
Any stabilization in demand could limit further downside volatility.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ee013c50b31f6e8dcd57:
Cobalt prices saw renewed interest from battery makers this week as buyers appeared to step up procurement ahead of scheduled demand, according to market participants and trading sources.

Several battery-related buyers signaled they were looking to add material for upcoming production cycles, narrowing the gap between offers and bid levels in parts of the market. Traders said the pickup was most evident in near-term deals, where limited availability and tighter lead times helped support pricing discussions.

In the spot market, sellers continued to emphasize availability constraints for certain cobalt products, including refined material used in lithium-ion battery supply chains. Buyers, facing delivery schedules tied to cell and pack assembly timelines, sought confirmation on shipment dates before committing quantities, sources said. Liquidity remained uneven, with trading concentrated among larger procurement desks.

Fastmarkets data and dealer accounts indicated that the tone improved after a period of subdued buying. One factor cited was a more stable view on how quickly end-demand from electric vehicle and electronics segments would translate into new orders for cathode components, although participants said forecasts remained sensitive to pricing and inventory levels.

Manufacturers of cathode active materials and battery intermediates were among the main parties engaging in the renewed procurement discussions, with some interest also reported from firms planning to replenish inventories. Traders said negotiations increasingly focused on specific grades and contractual terms, including freight arrangements, payment structures, and the scope of quality certification.

While the demand tone improved, the market remained influenced by broader supply conditions. Producers and traders said exports, refining throughput, and shipment timing continued to play a role in how quickly physical material could be delivered. Any shifts in supplier availability could affect the balance between buyers’ order patterns and sellers’ willingness to lower prices, they added.

“The buying interest has come back from battery-related customers, and that has helped support discussions for near-term volumes,” one metals trader said. “At the same time, most participants are still being selective about grade and delivery dates.”

Market participants said they would watch whether the renewed buying momentum extends beyond spot purchases into longer-term contracts, which typically help set expectations for subsequent pricing periods.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ccc5bc6581d531469c5a: Nickel prices are set to remain under pressure as global supply stays ahead of demand through year-end, according to market estimates circulated this week. Inventories at major exchanges and in trader warehouses have continued to build since late 2025, extending the period of oversupply into the first quarter of 2026. Fast-tracked production from Indonesia and other producing regions is expected to keep treatment and refining flows firm, with second-half volumes adding to already high stock levels. Demand growth remains constrained by slower stainless consumption and cautious procurement from battery supply chains ahead of 2027 contract renegotiations. Several benchmarks have softened over the last 10 to 12 weeks, reflecting expectations that near-term deficits are unlikely despite occasional production disruptions. Analysts cited a broad balance-of-risks skewed to surplus, with any tightening likely delayed until 4Q26, depending on closure schedules and grade mix in global ore output.
bodies_by_headline_hash.57dd65144433ab6e1ab2:
Copper prices on the LME tested the $9,500 resistance level as traders focused on tight physical availability and steady demand signals.  
The push toward the upper band came despite intermittent caution around global growth expectations and currency moves.  
Market participants said near-term buying interest remains concentrated in contracts linked to shorter lead times.  
Any acceptance above $9,500 could attract additional momentum flows, but sellers are watching for reversals if gains stall.  
Liquidity conditions around the resistance area are expected to be decisive for the next leg.  
Traders will also track macro data and China-related indicators for fresh direction.
bodies_by_headline_hash.8c1178c548dff083ef06:
The tin market tightened further this week as Indonesia’s exports remained limited, keeping supply pressure on global buyers, according to traders and market sources.

Benchmark prices and wider physical premiums reflected the reduced availability of cargoes, with sellers able to hold offers as inventory levels at trading hubs stayed lean. Several buyers reported longer lead times for prompt shipments and more selective delivery scheduling, particularly for product grades used in electronics and packaging.

Indonesia is the key exporter for refined tin, and market participants said uncertainty around export flows continued to constrain near-term supply. While shipments have resumed at times, the overall pace has not matched pre-tightening levels, leaving importers to bid more for available volumes. One trader said the tightening was visible in “both the prompt market and the distribution of cargoes,” as dealers focused on securing stock rather than expanding inventories.

In Singapore and other regional trading locations, liquidity remained stable but deals were described as increasingly dependent on the willingness of sellers to allocate limited tonnage. Traders said that when cargoes were offered, prices tended to clear quickly, leaving fewer opportunities for last-minute procurement. For contracts spanning the next several weeks, participants said the risk of delays increased, encouraging buyers to lock in supplies earlier than usual.

On the demand side, market sources said purchasing activity continued, but buyers were constrained by cost and the availability of acceptable substitutes. In the absence of additional supply, firms in downstream sectors such as solder and canmaking continued to negotiate to secure feedstock while maintaining production plans. Several participants noted that end-user purchases have been steady enough to absorb available tin, but not strong enough to prompt aggressive restocking beyond immediate needs.

Analysts also pointed to the role of inventories and refinancing in shaping the market. With stockrooms reportedly holding less material than in prior periods, trading firms have been reluctant to release inventory without support from firmer price expectations. This has contributed to tighter conditions across cash and short-dated contracts, they said.

Overall, the market’s near-term outlook remained centered on whether Indonesia’s export pace improves, and whether additional refiners or intermediaries can supply incremental volumes to relieve premiums. Until then, traders said the tin market is likely to stay sensitive to each incremental change in export documentation and shipping schedules.
bodies_by_headline_hash.9bda5035221d02fe0daf: Graphite flake suppliers warned on Monday that Q3 2026 will remain tight as mine output and conversion capacity fail to keep pace with demand from EV battery and industrial anode buyers. Several vendors said availability has tightened since late January, with lead times for commercial-grade flake extending from typical 4–8 weeks to about 8–12 weeks, depending on particle size and carbon content. The firms attributed the shortage to continued disruptions at a small number of processing sites, plus higher energy and maintenance costs that have limited run rates at purification and flake-splitting lines. Importers reported fewer spot offers and a shift toward fixed-quantity offtake contracts for loading windows in July–September. Price assessments are expected to remain volatile during the quarter, with upward pressure most likely in -80 mesh and -100 mesh grades, while larger-size flakes face slower adjustments. One supplier group forecast that by end-September inventories could stay below “normal” working levels in key customer regions, keeping pricing support through Q4 unless additional conversion capacity is commissioned.
bodies_by_headline_hash.8e3004145a47f6723cf8:
Platinum and palladium markets are moving in opposite directions as auto demand shifts across vehicle types.  
Platinum has shown resilience tied to broader industrial use and tighter emissions controls in parts of the sector.  
Palladium has diverged on expectations that automakers will keep accelerating substitution and refine fleet-wide purchasing patterns.  
Traders are also weighing inventory levels that remain uneven across trading hubs.  
Spot price signals are reflecting that split demand, with liquidity and order flow widening differently for each metal.  
Analysts say the next read-through will come from production updates and scrappage-driven purchasing cycles in key regions.  
Any further divergence could pressure long positions and reset near-term hedging strategies.
bodies_by_headline_hash.63cffde9cbc6b0bff369:
Tungsten spot premiums for European buyers widened this week, tracking firm buying demand and tighter availability of metal, according to market participants.

Dealers said the premium over benchmark levels for spot tungsten increased as sellers focused on fulfilling near-term contracts and reduced offers on shorter lead times. Several brokers linked the move to a combination of stronger orders from manufacturers preparing for scheduled production and a cautious approach to replenishing inventory, which left fewer lots available in the spot market.

In Europe, purchasers typically evaluate tungsten prices by referencing spot levels and adding a premium for immediate supply and sourcing conditions. Traders said the premium widened despite stable benchmark indications earlier in the month, reflecting a gap between physical availability and buyer demand rather than a broad move in the underlying reference price.

One market participant said tightness was concentrated in specific grades and packaging formats, which influenced how quickly deals could be executed. “The market is not uniformly tight, but the lots that are readily deliverable are commanding higher premiums,” the participant said.

Liquidity also appeared to thin for some contracts, with buyers reporting longer time-to-trade and fewer counterparty options. Several participants noted that sellers have shown more selectivity on counterparties and delivery terms, which can raise the effective premium even when benchmark assessments do not change materially.

Separately, sentiment remained influenced by expectations around industrial consumption and the sourcing plans of downstream users. Traders said suppliers were assessing incoming volumes from mining and processing streams before quoting additional spot material, contributing to uneven offers and supporting the widening premium.

The premium trend comes as European buyers continue to weigh short-term needs against longer-term supply arrangements. Brokers said some firms were negotiating for incremental quantities through spot transactions while maintaining portions of procurement through contracted channels, aiming to balance cost with certainty of delivery.
bodies_by_headline_hash.a8822c44ea6375c8fdf0: Vanadium prices stabilized in mid-March 2026 after a first-quarter drawdown that reduced sentiment across the ferrovanadium market. After sliding through January and February, benchmark offers for vanadium pentoxide and related products held steady during the week of March 9-15, with activity returning from a lower baseline volume. Market participants attributed the stabilization to restocking demand ahead of the second quarter and to tighter availability in several supplier lots, which had widened bid-ask spreads during February. Fastener, steelmaking and battery alloy buyers reduced destocking positions and shifted toward spot purchases, limiting further downward pressure. Participants also cited reduced unplanned secondary flows and longer lead times on some refining outputs as key balancing factors. Despite the stabilization, traders said pricing remains range-bound because buyers are still assessing demand for high-strength steel grades in the April-June period.
bodies_by_headline_hash.6981ed8682b1c69294ff:
Gallium markets remained broadly balanced after scrap shipments into Germany restarted, according to traders monitoring the metal’s spot and contract activity.  
Scrap flows helped stabilize availability for processors and reduced near-term pressure on refining margins.  
Several buyers said they were able to replenish inventories without paying sharp premiums, even as demand for semiconductors and LEDs continued to be selective.  
Pricing moved in a narrow band, with liquidity staying thin ahead of further confirmation on planned scrap import volumes.  
Participants noted that any interruption to German deliveries could quickly tighten supply given limited alternative sources.  
Overall sentiment was steady rather than bullish.
bodies_by_headline_hash.f46eafac7a4901db319b:
Indium prices rose to a two-year high as demand from semiconductor manufacturers supported tighter market conditions, traders and analysts said.

The metal, widely used in transparent conductive coatings and in indium-tin-oxide layers for displays and electronic components, reached its highest level in roughly two years amid improving order flow for semiconductor-related applications. Market participants said buyers have been working to secure material after a period of slower procurement, contributing to a stronger bid for available supply.

Several factors have been cited for the price advance, including firmer downstream demand and a gradual reduction in spot availability. Sellers have been more selective about discounting material, while inventories held by intermediaries have remained constrained, according to market sources. Suppliers in Asia and Europe have also faced pressure to balance contract deliveries with spot sales, limiting the amount of metal offered at lower price points.

In the semiconductor supply chain, indium is used in components where thin-film processing and high conductivity are needed, particularly in certain types of wafer and display manufacturing steps. Analysts said that as production schedules normalize, semiconductor orders have been translating into steadier procurement of indium-linked inputs. One commodities broker said the market has shifted toward “more active buying from end users,” which has helped lift prices without a major release of new inventories.

The move comes as broader conditions for industrial metals have remained mixed, with some segments supported by electronics demand and others affected by macroeconomic uncertainty. Even so, indium’s relatively narrow supply profile continues to make the market sensitive to changes in industrial consumption. A lack of meaningful substitute materials in certain applications has also limited the ability for buyers to switch away quickly.

Traders said monitoring will likely focus on whether semiconductor demand continues to strengthen and whether any additional supply emerges from smelters and processors. They also pointed to potential volatility around lead times and contract replenishment, which can influence spot availability week to week. For now, market participants expect prices to remain supported as long as procurement stays firm and inventories do not rebuild quickly.
bodies_by_headline_hash.48f69f51f312d47ebb1f: Molybdenum prices were steady on March 20 after market participants assessed news of a production outage in China that had initially raised short-term supply concerns. Traders said output disruptions at unspecified facilities were expected to be limited in duration, with spot availability in Asian markets remaining adequate for near-term orders. Several buyers reported mill inquiry activity continuing through the first week of March, supported by inventories at processors and downstream users who have been restocking after volatile moves earlier this quarter. Fastmarkets sources indicated that any impact would likely depend on how quickly concentrate grades and refining feedstock recover, particularly for contracts tied to March and April liftings. Benchmark molybdenum products traded in a narrow range despite the outage headline, reflecting reduced panic after similar incidents earlier in 2026. Market participants will monitor producer update dates later in the month, including revised operating rates and any follow-on maintenance schedules that could tighten supply into Q2.
bodies_by_headline_hash.0c03b980fe1a412c728e:
Uranium spot prices pushed up to test the $110 level as traders weighed fresh supply risks.  
Dealers pointed to tighter availability and slower procurement timelines across several contracts.  
Market participants said prompt buying interest strengthened, while seller offers remained selective.  
Confidence also shifted after renewed concerns about production disruptions and the pace of term supply renewal.  
Liquidity improved briefly, but price discovery stayed choppy as participants waited for confirmation from major utilities and enrichment counterparties.  
Analysts noted that even small changes in expectations can move spot levels quickly at current thin margins.  
Next signals to watch include inventory levels, contract rollovers, and any updates to mine and conversion schedules.
bodies_by_headline_hash.01fc497eb86f0884fe9b:
Cadmium prices were largely unchanged on Monday amid thin trading and subdued interest from buyers and sellers, according to market participants.

Deal activity remained limited as market liquidity thinned across the day, with participants citing cautious buying ahead of clearer demand signals from industrial customers. Several desks reported fewer inquiries and delayed price indications, reflecting a wait-and-see approach while counterparties assessed recent moves in downstream metals and battery-related supply chains.

In the physical market, traders said prompt availability continued to be offered at steady levels, but the lack of competitive bids kept the range narrow. Where offers were seen, they were generally close to prior reference points, and deals were described as sporadic rather than broad-based. The overall tone was quiet, with participants focusing on maintaining positions rather than initiating new commitments.

On the basis of reported transactions and inquiries, indications for cadmium products were largely flat across key maturities. Sellers appeared reluctant to lower prices without confirmation of further demand, while buyers maintained offers tied to tighter requirements and slower order intake. One trader said the market was “stuck waiting for incremental demand,” adding that the day’s thinness made it difficult to establish direction.

Market participants also pointed to currency and logistics considerations as factors affecting engagement. With shipments requiring time to line up and costs subject to change, counterparties were said to be prioritising terms and delivery windows over price aggressively. Some participants indicated that more activity might return as volume consolidates in the following sessions and as end-user buying requirements become clearer.

Despite the lack of movement, pricing continued to be monitored closely because cadmium is often linked to broader exposures in zinc smelting and to expectations around battery and pigment demand. Analysts said any shift in those underlying fundamentals could quickly affect sentiment, particularly in a market where trading depth remains limited.

For now, participants expect the market to remain range-bound unless trade flows improve and more confirmed deals emerge to set clearer benchmarks.
bodies_by_headline_hash.7e0e5dd0bf482882bc02: Premiums for selenium metal tightened as demand from South Korea eased, according to market participants tracking supply and pricing on March 21. Fastmarkets assessed the selenium premium at 8-10% over the reference level, down from 10-12% the week prior, reflecting slower buying activity tied to reduced procurement schedules in electronics and glass-related end uses. One trader said spot offers moved wider late in the week, with sellers attempting to maintain volumes despite thinner order books. Export fixtures into Korea reportedly slowed to about 20-25 tonnes for late March, versus 30-35 tonnes in the preceding period, based on deals reported across short-term contracts. Meanwhile, inventories held by processors in China were described as steady, limiting the ability of suppliers to lift premiums on the back of marginally firmer prices elsewhere in the supply chain. Market attention is expected to shift toward April demand indicators and any adjustments to smelter outputs following maintenance cycles scheduled across the next 4-6 weeks.
bodies_by_headline_hash.62b304590e3e452408c2:
Bismuth is set to face higher export costs in China.  
China plans an export tax increase starting in May.  
The rate will rise to 10%, according to market reporting.  
The change is likely to affect pricing and availability across global supply chains.  
Buyers may front-load purchases ahead of the May implementation date.  
Producers outside China could see demand shift toward alternative sourcing routes.  
Traders will watch for immediate adjustments in spot premiums and longer-dated contracts.  
The move also signals continued Chinese policy steering of raw materials exports.  
Market participants expect knock-on effects for downstream uses, including electronics and metallurgy.
bodies_by_headline_hash.a6a30539c0f5afa48568:
Antimony prices rose in recent trading as sanctions and compliance checks tightened the flow of the metal from Russia, traders and analysts said, lifting offers in spot and contract markets.

Global antimony supply has been increasingly disrupted by restrictions on Russian exports, including heightened screening by freight, insurance and bank counterparties, according to participants in the Asia and European markets. Several buyers said they have faced longer lead times and smaller shipment sizes as sellers navigate licensing requirements and customer verification processes.

In China, where antimony is widely processed and often used in flame retardants, batteries and alloys, sellers reportedly adjusted pricing after receiving fewer consignments that had previously supported inventories. One metals trader said the market has become more reliant on non-Russian feedstock and secondary material, which has helped support prices despite demand remaining mixed across end-use sectors.

“Spot availability has tightened, and that has pushed sellers to quote higher levels even where offtake is steady,” the trader said, requesting anonymity because of ongoing negotiations. The trader added that some buyers have shifted to longer-dated purchases to secure supply, contributing to firmer contract terms.

Market watchers said the rally is also linked to broader concerns about logistics continuity. Because antimony shipments are frequently routed through multiple intermediaries, delays in documentation and payment processing can extend the time from order to delivery. That has increased the risk premium embedded in offers, they said.

In Europe, traders reported that purchase decisions are being influenced by the pace of compliance approvals and the availability of certified material. Several buyers indicated they are seeking more guarantees on origin and handling, which can limit the pool of eligible suppliers and raise costs for screening and certification.

While spot premiums have firmed, analysts cautioned that the magnitude of the price move could depend on how quickly alternative supply routes ramp up. Additional volumes from other producing regions, coupled with any relaxation in payment or shipping friction, could reduce upward pressure. For now, however, participants said the sanctions-related bottleneck is still constraining Russian exports and supporting higher antimony values.
bodies_by_headline_hash.c467440116cb886985d8: A Germanium secondary smelter in Germany has scheduled maintenance work that will reduce output for several days in late March, according to an internal notice shared with customers. The plant plans a shutdown window starting 2026-03-28, with restart activity expected on 2026-04-02, subject to commissioning and test results. Operations tied to secondary feedstock processing, including sorting, leaching and refining steps, will be paused during the maintenance period, while limited utilities and inventory handling will continue. The smelter said affected shipments will be re-sequenced, with volumes prioritized for outstanding contracts scheduled before 03-28. Buyers are advised that lead times may extend by 5 to 10 business days for orders placed during the shutdown. The company did not specify maintenance scope, duration of mechanical work, or any production ramp details beyond the restart date.
bodies_by_headline_hash.a5c2e208b983c7ad95f7:
Beryllium prices inched higher after fresh U.S. defense demand expectations.  
Dealers reported firmer interest for specialty metal used in aerospace hardware and advanced electronics.  
Multiple participants said recent quote levels reflected tighter availability and longer lead times for processed grades.  
Spot discussions also pointed to cautious restocking by suppliers servicing government-linked programs.  
Market participants expect demand to stay resilient as suppliers work through existing qualification schedules.  
Any additional upward pressure could hinge on how quickly processors secure feedstock and machining capacity.  
Meanwhile, buyers are balancing higher costs against program timelines, limiting aggressive new commitments.
bodies_by_headline_hash.e761ba627d7c73a5ef4f:
The zirconium market was steady this week as buyers held back ahead of new order expectations from electric-vehicle original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), traders said, limiting spot activity and price discovery.

Dealers reported that inquiries for zirconium-containing materials remained more cautious than usual, with many participants waiting for clearer signals on EV supply contracts and downstream demand for components that use the metal or its compounds. While some processors continued shipping against existing commitments, fresh bookings were described as selective, with sellers prioritizing established customers.

In the wider market, commercial sentiment was largely shaped by uncertainty around the timing and scale of EV-related procurement, according to several industry sources. That caution was reflected in thinner order books and reduced negotiation activity, particularly for material grades used in specialty applications where qualification schedules can affect purchase timing.

A contributor to the quieter conditions was also the balance between inventory levels and near-term production schedules. Several suppliers said they could meet demand without urgent restocking, reducing the incentive to offer aggressive pricing. However, they noted that lead times for certain feedstocks and conversion steps could still create short, localized tightness if OEM orders move faster than expected.

“People are watching the next round of OEM discussions,” one European trader said. “Until there’s more certainty on volumes, most buyers prefer to wait and confirm requirements rather than add tonnage at current levels.”

Market participants said that any changes in automotive production plans could quickly translate into adjustments for zirconium procurement, given the metal’s role across high-performance manufacturing chains. Still, near-term signals were mixed, with some buyers indicating they were completing internal forecasting and qualification work that could precede formal purchase orders later in the quarter.

On pricing, traders said bids and offers largely tracked recent ranges, with limited movement due to the lack of large, market-moving deals. Most activity was characterized as incremental, consisting of smaller transactions tied to contract delivery schedules rather than spot-driven renegotiation.

The market’s next catalyst is expected to be guidance from EV OEMs and their tier-one suppliers on procurement schedules for the coming months, sources said. Until then, participants anticipate continued quiet trading as buyers and sellers align on volumes, specification requirements and delivery timelines.
bodies_by_headline_hash.164b3a5f169949dddc3e: Several lithium miners in Asia and Latin America guided for modestly higher output in the second quarter, adjusting quarterly production plans after supply chain easing and continued ramp-ups at existing brine and hard-rock sites. In preliminary guidance for Q2 2026, companies collectively pointed to volumes up in the low single digits versus the prior quarter, with improvement concentrated in spodumene concentrate shipments and brine conversion capacity. Dispatch schedules are expected to benefit from shorter port dwell times and more stable reagent deliveries during April and May, though weather disruption in mining regions remains a risk factor for June. Capex execution through the first quarter, including additional processing trains and upgraded evaporation ponds, is cited as the main driver behind the incremental lift. Demand for battery-grade lithium remains tied to consumer electronics and electric vehicle production plans, but firms said contract pricing remains volatile and could affect realized sales even if physical volumes rise. The updates follow tighter inventory management in January–February and a broader reassessment of processing recovery rates after 2025 quality variances.
bodies_by_headline_hash.8951f6a9812690937b1f:
Nickel mining economics in Indonesia are getting tighter as costs rise linked to higher nickel royalties.  
Several producers say the royalty changes increase cash costs at mine sites, cutting margins even when benchmark prices hold up.  
Industry participants expect the impact to be felt first in smaller operations and marginal ore grades.  
Bigger projects with stronger balance sheets may absorb part of the shock, but face pressure on expansion timelines.  
Companies are reviewing output schedules and logistics to offset higher payments.  
Analysts note the shift could also influence future investment and strengthen calls for renegotiated terms across the supply chain.
bodies_by_headline_hash.7e1b8f087e7444ff47e0:
Copper smelter treatment charges, or TCs, have risen to multi-year highs as tighter concentrate supply increases payments for raw material processing, according to industry benchmarks and participants.

The increases follow a period of production disruptions across major mining regions and a slower ramp-up of new concentrate capacity, leaving refiners competing more aggressively for feedstock. As a result, smelters are demanding higher compensation for processing copper concentrates into refined metal, a key component of how industrial buyers balance the economics of refining versus mining.

TCs reflect the fee paid by concentrate suppliers to smelters for converting concentrate to refined copper, with benchmark levels typically moving with global concentrate availability rather than the spot price for metal itself. When concentrate supply tightens, smelters can command higher fees because alternative processing options for producers become limited. Conversely, if concentrate supply loosens, TCs tend to fall.

Several traders and analysts said the latest pricing signals tighter physical supply conditions at the refinery level. “Higher treatment charges are a clear indication that smelter capacity is in demand relative to available concentrate,” one metals market participant said, adding that contract renegotiations have started to align with the improved smelter bargaining position.

The market has also been influenced by the pace of mine commissioning and declines in older operations that require maintenance or grade management. At the same time, logistics constraints and seasonal variations in freight have affected concentrate flows into refining hubs, according to multiple accounts from within the industry.

Smelter TCs are closely watched by both copper miners and refiners because they feed into the all-in cost structure for refined copper. Miners’ realized margins can improve when concentrate is scarce and TCs rise, while refiners face higher raw material processing costs if they cannot pass those fees through to customers quickly.

Looking ahead, market participants said near-term TC levels may remain supported as long as concentrate tightening persists and smelters maintain strict feedstock requirements. Any improvement in mine output or additional concentrates coming online could slow the pace of increases, but participants said the current tightness in supply has left prices sensitive to incremental changes in feed availability.
bodies_by_headline_hash.2dcc482001de2cfb9c67: Singapore iron ore futures rebounded after a 6% weekly slide, with gains extending into March 25 trade as sellers paused and bargain demand returned. The most-active contract rose on the day following heavy losses across the previous sessions, according to exchange data, after a week in which prices fell about 6% to mark the biggest decline in several recent weeks. Traders cited improving sentiment around industrial demand expectations and a stabilization in Chinese steel margins, which had weighed on sentiment earlier in the week. Physical market activity remained mixed, with some mills selectively buying prompt cargoes while others held back ahead of late-month restocking. Benchmark Dalian iron ore futures also firmed, though volumes stayed below mid-month averages, suggesting limited fresh risk-taking. Over the full week, however, sentiment remained fragile as macro signals continued to tug on commodity risk appetite, keeping price direction sensitive to daily import and steel output updates.
bodies_by_headline_hash.bfbf889b01f511123e02:
Chinese steelmakers are signaling softer margins and guiding second-quarter production lower.  
Several mills are expected to temper blast-furnace output as demand growth slows and price competition persists.  
Guidance points to a focus on maintaining cash flow rather than chasing volume.  
Analysts expect restocking demand to fade after earlier improvements in downstream buying.  
However, government support for industrial activity is likely to cap any sharp decline in consumption.  
Spot prices have remained under pressure, while costs have been mixed depending on coking coal and scrap inputs.  
Traders will watch follow-through on guidance and whether mills cut output enough to stabilize benchmarks.
bodies_by_headline_hash.1eab05a9a8d4f32bf4b3:
The European Union’s “IRA” policy framework has not yet materially shifted the outlook for lithium markets, according to analysts, who say the initiative is too recent and too narrow to overcome existing bottlenecks across mining, refining and battery supply chains.

While the IRA—referring to U.S. incentives under the Inflation Reduction Act—has driven interest in downstream battery projects, most of the spending that would meaningfully change lithium flows is still contingent on future eligibility rules, permitting timelines and construction schedules. Several analysts noted that the policy mostly affects the economics of finished components and batteries rather than accelerating upstream resource development.

Industry participants say the lithium sector faces structural constraints that incentives alone cannot resolve in the short term. New mine approvals, expansions of processing capacity and upgrades to convert raw concentrates into battery-grade lithium require multiyear investment and regulatory review. “Even with demand-side incentives, the bottleneck remains supply-side capacity, particularly the refining step,” said one analyst at a commodities research firm, pointing to delays in construction and commissioning.

The analysis also highlights that eligibility requirements for IRA-linked tax credits depend on specific sourcing and production thresholds for battery materials. Those conditions can divert investment toward projects in certain geographies and with qualifying partners, but they do not automatically translate into immediate new offtake volumes. Several projects have announced plans to meet the criteria, but contracts often require completed capacity and proof of production, creating a lag between policy announcements and physical supply changes.

Market data and corporate filings indicate that lithium prices and contract volumes have been influenced more by global production decisions and regional demand than by any single policy shift. Several producers have prioritized cash preservation and phased expansions amid volatility in spot pricing, while processors have faced challenges in securing feedstock and meeting quality specifications at scale.

Analysts said the impact of the IRA is more likely to be felt through incremental changes to contracting patterns over time, including longer-term offtakes for qualifying material and new investments in chemical conversion and refining capacity. They expect a clearer assessment after more IRA-certified supply chains come online and after regulators provide additional guidance on compliance and documentation for credit qualification.
bodies_by_headline_hash.4397557aa5a7aed57a49: Cobalt production and trading remain dominated by the Democratic Republic of the Congo, despite repeated market claims that supplies are diversifying, according to industry data and recent policy moves. In 2025 and early 2026, the DRC continued to account for the majority of global mined cobalt output, with most incremental growth tied to artisanal and industrial processing in Katanga and adjacent regions. Administrative and security disruptions have repeatedly affected shipment timing, while audits of mining sites have highlighted uneven compliance with child-labour and traceability requirements across cooperatives. Traders and refiners have said they can source more material from partner jurisdictions, but refining capacity and battery-grade specifications still concentrate near Congo-linked feedstock. Market commentary over the past 12 months has also been shaped by export licensing changes, periodic suspensions in key zones, and fluctuating export volumes that have translated into price volatility at the LME and in Asian offtake contracts. The cobalt story therefore remains centered on DRC governance, enforcement, and supply reliability as the primary swing factors for investors and manufacturers.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ef6f38ca4232c6fa0457:
Copper is trading less like a metal and more like a bond proxy lately.  
Prices are moving with real yields and the dollar instead of only industrial demand signals.  
When investors expect slower growth or easier policy, copper can rally even without immediate pick-up in physical consumption.  
That behavior reflects positioning flows through macro funds, where copper is a liquid, scalable way to express rate and risk sentiment.  
At the same time, supply discipline and inventory swings still matter, but they are being overshadowed by discount-rate effects.  
Traders are watching yield moves, China credit conditions, and whether the move persists across futures curves.
bodies_by_headline_hash.5eaf416d6286b4f16554:
Rare earths have drawn renewed investor attention this quarter, but lithium has continued to dominate traded volumes as supply chains for electric vehicles and energy storage ramp up, according to market participants and recent industry updates.

In commodity markets, headlines have increasingly focused on rare earths such as neodymium and dysprosium, which are used in high-performance magnets for wind turbines, electric motors and some defense systems. Traders have pointed to periodic supply disruptions, tighter export controls in parts of Asia and ongoing efforts by governments and automakers to diversify magnet supply.

That attention has not translated into comparable liquidity, however. Rare earths markets remain thinner than benchmark metal contracts, with pricing often driven by bilateral deals, spot tenders and index methodologies that can vary by source. “Rare earths are where the policy and strategic narratives are landing, but the trading volume is still smaller because the physical market is more fragmented,” said one London-based metals analyst familiar with exchange activity, speaking on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to comment publicly.

By contrast, lithium continues to see broader participation from producers, converters and battery materials buyers, which has supported higher transaction volumes in both physical and derivatives-linked products. Markets have been shaped by a continuing shift toward longer-duration battery deployment and a steady pipeline of projects for spodumene conversion and new lithium hydroxide capacity, participants said.

Recent price movements for lithium have been influenced by expectations for battery demand growth and by the pace of new supply coming online from new and expanded operations. While individual producers have faced challenges ranging from permitting delays to operational disruptions, the overall market has remained active, with buyers tracking contract renegotiations and spot availability.

Analysts said the coexistence of rare earths’ headline visibility and lithium’s trading depth reflects differences in market structure. Rare earths tend to be less standardized and more dependent on magnet-grade specs, while lithium products are increasingly traded at scale through established benchmarking and contracting conventions linked to battery chemistries.

Market watchers said both categories could remain in focus as companies work to secure inputs for domestic manufacturing and as governments continue to review critical minerals strategies. For now, rare earths are drawing strategic attention, while lithium continues to be the center of volume as battery demand remains the most liquid driver across the wider battery metals complex.
bodies_by_headline_hash.d1855a7740f73b8c4acf: A Reuters investigation provides an inside look at a Ganfeng Lithium processing site, highlighting how the company turns brine and hard-rock feedstock into battery-grade material on an industrial scale. The facility, located in China, runs multi-stage production that includes chemical refining, impurity removal, and crystallization, with batch cycles and continuous monitoring designed to keep key parameters within tight tolerances. Over the past year, Ganfeng has expanded capacity for lithium intermediates and finished salts, reflecting rising demand tied to electric-vehicle and grid-storage orders. In the processing lines, operators track concentrations, reagent consumption, and waste-water treatment performance, with magnesium, calcium, and sulfate managed through controlled precipitation and filtration steps. The report describes standardized maintenance schedules for pumps and evaporators, alongside laboratory testing that checks purity and particle characteristics before material proceeds to downstream refining. Energy use and emissions controls are integrated into the workflow, including heat-recovery systems and scrubbers, as regulators tighten requirements for industrial effluent and air pollutants through 2024 and 2025.
bodies_by_headline_hash.c857e2ebf1e184aaf2b1:
Lithium spot pricing kept its six-week pattern, slipping again after a brief rebound.  
After improving earlier, the next leg down reflected softer immediate buying interest.  
Traders pointed to thin urgency on the physical market, with buyers waiting for better offers.  
Supply remained available enough to cap any sustained recovery.  
Market participants said the up-down rhythm suggests continued uncertainty over demand timing.  
Some participants linked the hesitation to broader battery supply chain caution ahead of upcoming procurement cycles.  
With sentiment remaining fragile, each bounce looks more like a correction than a reversal.  
Until demand improves, spot levels may keep rotating within the same range.
bodies_by_headline_hash.9db02f69e53114b91cdc:
A chart circulated by Rare Earth Watch shows cobalt export volumes from the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), with a moving average used to smooth short-term fluctuations over time.

The graphic, shared via Telegram, plots export volumes and overlays a moving average line, indicating the trend rather than day-to-day movement. The chart is presented as a data visualization for market watchers following developments in the DRC’s mining and export activity, a key source of global cobalt used in batteries.

In the period covered by the chart, the moving-average line shows periods of increase and decline, suggesting that export flows have not moved in a straight line. The change in the average appears to reflect shifts in shipments, rather than a single abrupt change, the chart’s structure suggests. The underlying series is displayed alongside the smoothing calculation, allowing viewers to compare raw movement with the trend line.

The Telegram post includes the chart without accompanying figures in the text, and it does not provide context on what specific export categories or reporting channels were used for the input data. The visualization nevertheless highlights timing patterns, with the moving average indicating when export volumes likely accelerated or slowed relative to earlier points in the series.

Market analysts and battery supply-chain participants closely track DRC export statistics because the country’s large share of mined cobalt influences both pricing expectations and downstream production planning. Cobalt supply can be affected by operational conditions at mines, logistics constraints, and administrative changes that influence how and when shipments leave producing regions.

The moving-average approach is commonly used in commodity tracking to reduce noise caused by uneven reporting, temporary shipment delays, and fluctuations in weekly or monthly totals. By smoothing the series, the chart aims to make the direction of change easier to interpret for readers monitoring how export levels are evolving.

Rare Earth Watch did not immediately respond to requests for additional detail about the dataset methodology or the time period shown in the graphic. The chart continues to circulate among viewers tracking cobalt supply trends from the DRC.
bodies_by_headline_hash.725fe93c8ca78d0ca0eb: London Metal Exchange nickel stockpiles are moving out of sync with spot pricing, according to market data cited by traders tracking the divergence. As of 29 March 2026, LME cash inventories were reported at levels down versus the opening weeks of March, with warrants and customer holdings shifting between buckets amid uneven demand signals. Over the same period, nickel prices were seen weakening in late March even as inventories continued to decline, a pattern that typically suggests futures-led hedging rather than immediate physical tightness. One factor highlighted by analysts is the persistence of multi-week backwardation in nearby contracts, which can encourage inventory drawdowns while discouraging higher cash bids. Positioning flows into March and early April were also said to be influenced by calendar spread roll activity and wider moves in broader base metals, complicating any clean read-through from stock levels to price. Traders said follow-through will depend on whether inventory reductions persist beyond the first half of April and whether cancellations or re-warrants accelerate.
bodies_by_headline_hash.45762a9ae64c7a4e6546:
Uranium prices saw a sharp post-event move that quickly faded.  
Early buying lifted sentiment, but follow-through was thin.  
Volatility collapsed after the initial reaction, signaling reduced urgency among traders.  
That pattern often points to positioning changes rather than a new, durable supply-demand shift.  
Market participants appear to be waiting for the next confirmed catalyst: contract news, utility procurement schedules, or policy signals.  
Liquidity looks deeper, with fewer exaggerated swings than during the lead-up period.  
For now, the tape suggests the market is absorbing the event and resetting expectations.
bodies_by_headline_hash.e92bd312afb49cf3798c:
Rare-earth prices were largely steady over the past week, with benchmark values for several key materials remaining unchanged, according to a roundup compiled from market tracking posted on Telegram.

The weekly snapshot showed no movement in select reference prices for a group of rare metals used in magnets, catalysts and electronics. Across the reported period, buyers and sellers held to existing levels for certain heavier rare earths, including dysprosium and terbium, both of which are closely watched for supply risk and demand tied to high-performance permanent magnets.

Other materials in the report also showed stability. Praseodymium and neodymium, the main rare-earth inputs for NdFeB magnet production, were assessed at unchanged levels week-on-week. The same was reported for lanthanum, a lanthanide used in polishing powders and catalysts, as well as for cerium, which is widely used in industrial applications including fuel refining and abrasives.

Among the less commonly traded metals tracked in the roundup, yttrium likewise posted no change over the week. The report did not cite any specific transactions or spot deals that drove the lack of movement, instead indicating that prevailing pricing expectations remained intact across the reference set.

The roundup also reflected that thin liquidity and limited spot activity can keep prices pinned for extended periods, particularly for products that trade more through negotiated contracts than public exchanges. It offered no information on changes in inventories, shipment schedules or downstream orders that might have triggered a reassessment of the benchmarks.

A representative for the tracking outlet said that the steady print reflected a “wait-and-see” stance among buyers ahead of clearer signals from end-use demand and ongoing discussions with suppliers. “For this week, there was no new data that warranted adjustments,” the representative said.

The report’s emphasis on unchanged levels suggests that the market is currently balancing ongoing interest in secure supplies with cautious procurement. Prices may remain range-bound until new information emerges on feedstock availability, refining capacity, or purchase commitments from magnet and catalyst producers, the roundup indicated.
bodies_by_headline_hash.fcd2c835d1686a6df7b5:
MP Materials published its quarterly operations summary, providing an update on mining and processing activity at its U.S. rare earths operations.

In the filing, the company said it continued to operate its mine and processing facilities in Mountain Pass, California, where it produces rare earth concentrates and processes material into separated products used by magnet and other industrial applications. The summary outlines throughput and production metrics for the quarter, along with operational notes related to equipment performance and processing yields.

MP Materials also described inventory movements and the status of work-in-progress during the period, including how material is handled between refining stages. The company said it manages production and staging to support downstream separation activities, while also aligning output with the timing of product deliveries and shipment schedules.

The company’s update included information on electricity consumption and other operating inputs used at the site, as well as references to ongoing maintenance activities. MP Materials said it continues to monitor key process parameters to maintain stable performance and to reduce disruptions that can affect production rates at different stages of the processing flow.

Separately, the summary discussed progress related to its long-term capacity plans and the ramp-up of refining and separation capabilities. MP Materials reiterated that its operations depend on the availability of inputs, plant uptime, and the performance of separation and finishing steps that convert concentrates into separated rare earth products.

A spokesperson for MP Materials said in the summary that the company’s production results reflected steady operations during the quarter, with management focused on maintaining throughput and product quality while executing routine maintenance and process optimization. The company did not provide additional guidance figures in the operations update, according to the filing.

MP Materials’ quarterly operations summary is intended to complement its financial reporting by offering more granular details on operational performance at its U.S. site. The company said it will continue to provide periodic updates in future quarters as it advances its commercial production plans for rare earth materials.
bodies_by_headline_hash.a1dc7622f46022ebfa93: Fastmarkets published selected findings from its annual industry survey covering the rare-earths market on March 15, 2026, with data compiled across spot and contract activity from January 2025 through December 2025. The release highlights pricing movements for neodymium and praseodymium at major trade hubs, reporting monthly averages alongside end-of-quarter settlement levels for benchmark lots. According to the compiled points, supply tightness eased in Q3 2025 relative to Q2, while demand indicators from magnet and metal-alloy segments remained uneven through September 2025. The survey also lists changes in downstream procurement terms, including lead-time revisions that shifted median ordering schedules by roughly 2 to 4 weeks during the last two quarters of the year. For exporters, the selected dataset includes shipment-size distributions and route-level disruptions tied to port congestion observed between July and October 2025. Fastmarkets said additional tables covering dysprosium and terbium, plus refined oxide conversions and contract index behavior, will follow in later releases.
bodies_by_headline_hash.d5564a0bebf710f64b78:
Trade publications tracked by industry participants this week highlighted continued pressure on rare-earth supply chains, with several notes pointing to tighter availability for specific materials and renewed attention to pricing and contracting practices.

One report focused on the market’s ongoing segmentation, where demand for high-performance magnets and specialty applications is shaping purchasing patterns. Several sources said buyers have continued to favor multi-month arrangements and pre-booking for dysprosium and terbium-containing products, while spot deals remain comparatively limited. The reports said uncertainty over downstream inventories has led many traders to treat near-term availability as more volatile than it was earlier in the cycle.

Another roundup item noted that producers and processors are balancing output with compliance requirements and export controls, which can affect lead times for refined and separated rare-earths. Market participants said that even when mine supply is stable, processing capacity for separation and refining can become the binding constraint. This, in turn, is influencing contract terms, including delivery schedules and quality specifications for mixed rare-earth streams.

A separate trade note examined developments in magnet supply, where manufacturers are monitoring the risk of input substitution and grade adjustments. It said magnet makers are reviewing sourcing strategies for heavy rare-earths used in high-temperature and high-coercivity applications. Several industry contacts indicated that conversion to alternative compositions can be commercially constrained by qualification timelines with customers, leaving some producers more exposed to price swings than others.

The roundup also pointed to ongoing efforts to expand and secure feedstock for rare-earth separation, including through joint ventures and long-term offtake agreements. It said investment timelines and permitting processes remain critical variables, particularly for projects seeking to bring separated output online. In the meantime, traders said companies are managing working capital by rotating inventories and narrowing the range of grades they purchase to match downstream requirements.

In one attributed comment, a rare-earth market analyst said the current environment is defined less by broad shortages across all rare-earths and more by “specific-material bottlenecks that show up in contracting and lead times.” The analyst added that buyers appear to be responding by tightening procurement discipline and focusing on assured delivery windows.

Overall, the trade press coverage suggested that while overall sentiment remains cautious, the market’s near-term direction will be determined by the pace of separation capacity utilization, the terms of existing contracts, and the degree of volatility in heavy rare-earth availability.
